title: Case on National Greenhouse Gas Reduction Targets Addressing the Climate Crisis
text:
- 54 -Case on National Greenhouse Gas Reduction Targets Addressing 
the Climate Crisis
[2020Hun-Ma389, 2021Hun-Ma1264 (consolidated), 2022Hun-Ma85 4 
(consolidated), 2023Hun-Ma846 (consolidated), August 29, 2024]
Complainants
Complainants listed in Appendix 1
Intervenor
Intervenor listed in Appendix 1
Decided
August 29, 2024
Holding
1. Article 8, Section (1) of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality 
and Green Growth for Coping with Climate Crisis (enacted by Act No. 
18469 on September 24, 2021) does not conform to the Constitution. 
The said statutory provision shall remain in effect until it is amended by 
February 28, 2026.
2. The claim challenging Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement 
Decree (enacted by Presidential Decree No. 32557 on March 25, 2022) 
of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth for 
Coping with Climate Crisis, and the claims challenging both “B. Sectoral 
Reduction Targets” and “C. Annual Reduction Targets” under “V. Mid- 
to Long-Term Reduction Targets” in the First National Basic Plan for 
Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth, established by the government on 
April 11, 2023, are all rejected. 
3.The remaining claims of Complainants and the motions of 
Intervenor to intervene as a Complainant and to intervene in support of 
- 55 -Complainants are all dismissed as nonjusticiable.
Reasoning
I. Overview of Case
A. Case No. 2020Hun-Ma389
1. Complainants are individuals born between February 15, 2001 and 
November 6, 2006 and are members of a youth environmental organization 
called “Youth 4 Climate Action.”
2. On March 13, 2020, Complainants filed the instant constitutional 
complaint, asserting that the following infringed, inter alia, their right to 
life, environmental right, and right to equality, in the context of setting 
greenhouse gas reduction targets to address the climate crisis: i) Article 
42, Section (1), Item 1 of the former Framework Act on Low Carbon, 
Green Growth (hereinafter referred to as the “former Green Growth Act” 
unless otherwise specified), which required the government to set 
greenhouse gas reduction targets; ii) the act of the President in amending 
Article 25, Section (1) of the former Green Growth Act Enforcement 
Decree on May 24, 2016, thereby changing the total national greenhouse 
gas emissions reduction target from a 2020-based target to a 2030-based 
target; and iii) the amendment of Article 25, Section (1) of the former 
Green Growth Act Enforcement Decree on December 31, 2019, which 
defined the “target for the reduction of greenhouse gases” under Article 
42, Section (1), Item 1 of the same Act as a reduction of the total national 
greenhouse gas emissions in 2030 by 244 per 1,000 of the total national 
greenhouse gas emissions in 2017.
3. Complainants subsequently submitted two applications to supplement 
the claims and grounds in their constitutional complaint: iv) on February 
16, 2022, they added as a Provision at Issue Article 8, Section (1) of the 
- 56 -Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth for Coping 
with Climate Crisis (hereinafter referred to as the “Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act” unless otherwise specified), which provides that the 
government shall set a “mid- to long-term national greenhouse gas 
reduction target” to reduce national greenhouse gas emissions by a ratio 
prescribed by Presidential Decree within the range of not less than 35 
percent from the 2018 level by 2030; and v) on June 8, 2022, they added 
as a further Provision at Issue Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement 
Decree of the same Act, which sets the “ratio prescribed by Presidential 
Decree” under the above provision at 40 percent. Complainants asserted 
that each of these provisions violates the State’s duty to protect 
fundamental rights, disproportionately infringes the fundamental rights of 
future generation Complainants, and results in discriminatory treatment.
B. Case No. 2021Hun-Ma1264
On October 12, 2021, Complainants filed the instant constitutional 
complaint against Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act, asserting that the greenhouse gas reduction target under 
this provision is insufficient to protect their lives and safety from climate 
disasters, as required under internationally agreed standards such as the 
Paris Agreement. They contended that the target fails to constitute an 
appropriate and effective minimum measure to protect their rights to life, 
to the pursuit of happiness, to the general freedom of action, to live in 
a healthy and pleasant environment, to freedom, and to equality, thereby 
infringing their fundamental rights. 
C. Case No. 2022Hun-Ma854
1. Complainants consist of a fetus who had not yet been born at the 
time of filing the instant complaint and individuals born between January 
4, 2012 and March 25, 2022.
- 57 -2. On June 13, 2022, Complainants filed the instant constitutional 
complaint against Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, arguing that the greenhouse gas 
reduction target set by this provision is insufficient under international 
standards such as the Paris Agreement to protect their lives and safety 
from climate disasters. They contended that the provision fails to 
constitute an appropriate and effective minimum measure to safeguard 
their rights to life, to the pursuit of happiness, to general freedom of 
action, to live in a healthy and pleasant environment, to equality, and to 
property, thereby infringing their fundamental rights. 
3. Complainant who had been a fetus at the time of filing was born 
on October 6, 2022 and was subsequently identified as C.H. in the case 
record.
D. Case No. 2023Hun-Ma846 
1. Pursuant to Article 10 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, the 
government formulated the “First National Basic Plan for Carbon 
Neutrality and Green Growth,” which was finalized on April 11, 2023, 
following deliberation by the “2050 Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth 
Commission” on April 10 and deliberation by the State Council on the 
following day.
2. In “V. Mid- to Long-Term Reduction Targets,” of the above Basic 
Plan, “B. Sectoral Reduction Targets” sets the national greenhouse gas 
emissions target for 2030 at 436.6 million tons of carbon dioxide 
equivalent (tCO₂-eq, hereinafter the same), based on net emissions, 
which refers to gross emissions minus absorption and removal, in 
accordance with Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree of 
the same Act, by reducing 40 percent from the 2018 level. The Plan 
also sets the 2030 sectoral targets for emissions and absorption, dividing 
- 58 -them into emission sectors —such as energy conversion, industry, 
buildings, transportation, agriculture-livestock-fisheries, waste, hydrogen, 
and fugitive emissions —and into absorption and removal sectors, 
including sinks, CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization and Storage), and 
international reduction.
3. In “V. Mid- to Long-Term Reduction Targets,” of the above Basic 
Plan, “C. Annual Reduction Targets” sets the total net emissions and 
sectoral emissions for the base year 2018 and the annual targets for total 
net emissions, sectoral emissions, and absorption from 2023 through 
2030.
4.In “VII. Fiscal Plan and Expected Effects,” “1. Fiscal Investment 
Plan” sets forth a fiscal investment plan of at least 89.9 trillion won 
from 2023 to 2027 to support carbon neutrality and green growth.
5.On July 6, 2023, Complainants filed the instant constitutional 
complaint, asserting that the Basic Plan sets targets that are insufficient to 
protect their lives and safety from climate disasters under internationally 
agreed standards such as the Paris Agreement. They argued that the 
targets fail to constitute appropriate and efficient minimum measures to 
safeguard their rights to life, the pursuit of happiness, general freedom of 
action, equality, property, and to live in a healthy and pleasant 
environment, thereby infringing their fundamental rights.
E.Motions to Intervene as Complainant and in Support of Complainants 
On February 15, 2024, the Court consolidated the cases described in 
paragraphs B. through D. into case 2020Hun-Ma389, described in 
paragraph A. Subsequently, on May 29, 2024, Movant filed, with respect 
to the consolidated constitutional complaint, a primary motion to 
intervene as a Complainant and, in the alternative, a motion to intervene 
in support of Complainants.
- 59 -II. Subject Matter of Review 
A. Complainants in case 2020Hun-Ma389 challenged, as part of the 
subject matter of review, the “abolishment of the 2020 greenhouse gas 
reduction target by the President.” This challenge pertains to the 
amendment on May 24, 2016, of Article 25, Section (1) of the former 
Green Growth Act Enforcement Decree, which originally, before the 
amendment, had set the greenhouse gas reduction target under Article 
42, Section (1), Item 1 of the former Green Growth Act as a target level 
of total national greenhouse gas emissions for 2020 up to 30 percent 
below the projected level for that year. The amendment revised this 
target to a level of total national greenhouse gas emissions for 2030 up 
to 37 percent below the projected level for that year. Accordingly, the 
provision of the Enforcement Decree as amended on May 24, 2016 is 
included in the subject matter of review in this regard.
B. Complainants in case 2023Hun-Ma846 filed a constitutional 
complaint challenging the entirety of the “First National Basic Plan for 
Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth,” which had been finalized and 
adopted by the government through deliberation and resolution of the 
State Council on April 11, 2023. However, as the alleged grounds for 
infringement of fundamental rights relate to matters such as the sectoral 
allocation of reduction targets, the figures and reduction pathways of 
annual reduction targets, and deficiencies in the fiscal planning, the 
subject matter of review is limited to those parts of the Basic Plan that 
pertain to the above.
C. Therefore, the subject matter of review in this case is whether the 
following provisions and measures infringe Complainants’ fundamental 
rights:
i)Article 42, Section (1), Item 1 of the former Framework Act on 
Low Carbon, Green Growth (enacted by Act No. 9931 on January 
- 60 -13, 2010 and before repealed by Act No. 18469 on September 24, 
2021);
ii)Article 25, Section (1) of the former Framework Act on Low 
Carbon, Green Growth Enforcement Decree (amended by 
Presidential Decree No. 27180 on May 24, 2016 and before 
amendment by Presidential Decree No. 30303 on December 31, 
2019; hereinafter referred to as the “2016 Green Growth Act 
Enforcement Decree”);
iii)Article 25, Section (1) of the former Framework Act on Low Carbon, 
Green Growth Enforcement Decree (amended by Presidential 
Decree No. 30303 on December 31, 2019 and before repealed by 
Presidential Decree No. 32557 on March 25, 2022; hereinafter 
referred to as the “2019 Green Growth Act Enforcement Decree”);
iv)Article 8, Section (1) of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality 
and Green Growth for Coping with Climate Crisis (enacted by Act 
No. 18469 on September 24, 2021);
v)Article 3, Section (1) of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality 
and Green Growth for Coping with Climate Crisis Enforcement 
Decree (enacted by Presidential Decree No. 32557 on March 25, 
2022);
vi)Sections B and C, titled “Sectoral Reduction Targets” and “Annual 
Reduction Targets,” respectively, of Part V, “Mid- to Long-Term 
Reduction Targets,” of the First National Basic Plan for Carbon 
Neutrality and Green Growth, established by the government on 
April 11, 2023 (hereinafter referred to as the “Basic Plan at Issue”) 
(hereinafter referred to as the “Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue” 
and the “Annual Reduction Targets at Issue,” respectively, and 
collectively as the “Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue”); and
vii)Section 1, “Fiscal Investment Plan,” of Part VII, “Fiscal Plan and 
- 61 -Expected Effects,” of the Basic Plan at Issue (hereinafter referred to as 
the “Fiscal Plan at Issue”; vi) and vii), collectively, the “Plans at 
Issue”)
The Provisions at Issue and related provisions are as set forth below, 
and the Plans at Issue are attached in Appendix 2.
[Provisions at Issue]

(2)The Minister of Environment shall exercise general supervision 
over and coordinate the affairs regarding the setting and modification of 
the national mid- to long-term greenhouse gas reduction targets, sectoral

as “mid- to long-term greenhouse gas reduction targets, etc.”).
(4)Where the government sets, modifies, or resets the mid- to

main clause of Article 35 (3) of the Act.
- 66 -III. Arguments of Complainants and Opinions of Interested Institutions
The arguments of Complainants and the opinions of interested 
institutions are as set forth in Appendix 3.
IV. Justiciability of Motions for Intervention
A. Motion to Intervene as Complainant
In a constitutional complaint against a statute, regulation, etc., if the 
outcome of the adjudication must be conclusively and uniformly 
determined for both the complainant and a third party, the third party may 
participate in the adjudication as a complainant pursuant to Article 83, 
Section (1) of the Civil Procedure Act as applied mutatis mutandis under 
Article 40, Section (1) of the Constitutional Court Act. However, because 
an intervenor joins the pending adjudication as a complainant in lieu of 
filing a separate constitutional complaint, the motion to intervene as a 
complainant must be filed within the filing period for a constitutional 
complaint (see, inter alia, Constitutional Court 2021Hun-Ma1379, 
September 26, 2023; Constitutional Court 2023Hun-Ma105, April 25, 
2024).
Movant raises issues concerning correctional treatment in relation to 
adaptation to climate change. Thus, it is difficult to regard Movant as 
holding a legal status that requires a conclusive and uniform determination 
in connection with the outcome of the instant constitutional complaint, 
which concerns greenhouse gas reduction as a mitigation measure against 
climate change.
Moreover, among the Provisions at Issue, Article 42, Section (1), Item 
1 of the former Green Growth Act took effect on April 14, 2010; Article 
25, Section (1) of the 2016 former Green Growth Act Enforcement 
Decree on June 1, 2016; Article 25, Section (1) of the 2019 former 
- 67 -Green Growth Act Enforcement Decree on December 31, 2019; and 
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree on March 25, 2022 (see 
Article 1, main clause of the former Framework Act on Low Carbon 
Green Growth Addendum [Act No. 9931, January 13, 2010]; Article 1 of 
the same Act’s Enforcement Decree Addendum [Presidential Decree No. 
27180, May 24, 2016]; the same Act’s Enforcement Decree Addendum 
[Presidential Decree No. 30303, December 31, 2019]; Article 1, main 
clause of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth for 
Coping with Climate Crisis Addendum [Act No. 18469, September 24, 
2021]; and Article 1, main clause of the same Act’s Enforcement Decree 
Addendum [Presidential Decree No. 32557, March 25, 2022]). The Plans 
at Issue were established and implemented on April 11, 2023. Movant 
filed a motion to intervene as a Complainant on May 29, 2024, after the 
one-year filing period had elapsed for these Plans.
Accordingly, Movant’s motion to intervene as a Complainant is 
nonjusticiable.
B. Motion to Intervene in Support of Complainants
Movant, in the alternative, filed a motion to intervene in support of 
Complainants. A third party who has an interest in the outcome of a 
constitutional complaint may intervene in the pending constitutional 
complaint in support of either party (see, inter alia, Constitutional Court 
Act, Article 40, Section (1); Civil Procedure Act, Article 71, main 
clause; Constitutional Court 2023Hun-Ma105, April 25, 2024). 
However, as stated in relation to the above motion to intervene as a 
Complainant, Movant’s claim pertains to correctional treatment and does 
not appear to give rise to a legal interest in the outcome of the instant 
constitutional complaint. 
Accordingly, Movant’s motion to intervene in support of Complainants 
is nonjusticiable.
- 68 -V.Analysis of Article 42, Section (1), Item 1 of the Former Green 
Growth Act and Article 25, Section (1) of 2016 and 2019 Former 
Green Growth Act Enforcement Decrees
A. Cognizable Interest
When a provision of law that was the subject of a constitutional 
complaint is amended after the complaint is filed, thereby eliminating the 
possibility of its further application to the complainant, the complainant’s 
cognizable interest in obtaining a ruling of unconstitutionality with respect 
to the challenged provision is extinguished absent special circumstances. 
In such cases, the constitutional complaint is nonjusticiable (see, inter 
alia, Constitutional Court 2007Hun-Ma103, April 30, 2009).
However, an exception may be recognized where there is a risk of 
repeated infringement of fundamental rights, or where the resolution of 
the dispute is essential for maintaining and protecting the constitutional 
order and thus carries significant constitutional meaning (see, inter alia, 
Constitutional Court 91Hun-Ma44, May 25, 1995). If the constitutionality 
of the prior provision has not yet been clarified, the amended provision 
contains similar content, and the risk of similar infringements of 
fundamental rights persists, and if determining the constitutionality of the 
prior provision may influence a future amendment of the current 
provision, then clarifying the constitutionality of the prior provision still 
carries substantial significance. In such a case, an interest may be deemed 
cognizable (see, inter alia, Constitutional Court 2001Hun-Ma565, May 
15, 2003).
B. Assessment
Statutes and regulations that establish greenhouse gas reduction targets 
pertain to national goals and plans premised on a future point in time. If 
such targets are amended and reset before that point arrives, the repealed 
- 69 -targets no longer have any potential to affect the rights of the people, 
including Complainants, or the conduct of State organs.
The “target for the reduction of greenhouse gases” set forth in Article 
42, Section (1), Item 1 of the former Green Growth Act and Article 25, 
Section (1) of the 2016 and 2019 former Green Growth Act 
Enforcement Decrees were repealed upon the respective entry into force 
of Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree on March 25, 2022 
(Article 1, main clause, Article 2 of the Addendum to the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act [Act No. 18469, September 24, 2021]; Article 
1, main clause and Article 2, Section (2) of the Addendum to the 
Enforcement Decree of the same Act [Presidential Decree No. 32557, 
March 25, 2022]). Pursuant to Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement 
Decree, the State’s mid- to long-term greenhouse gas reduction target 
was amended and reset to reduce national greenhouse gas emissions by 
40 percent by 2030 compared to 2018 levels. Therefore, the former 
provisions are no longer applicable to citizens including Complainants.
Complainants argue that the greenhouse gas reduction targets under the 
Provisions at Issue are insufficient. However, the new targets under 
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree, which replaced Article 
42, Section (1), Item 1 of the former Green Growth Act and Article 25, 
Section (1) of the 2016 and 2019 former Enforcement Decrees, raised 
the reduction standard by lowering the post-reduction emissions target 
for 2030 from 536.0 million tons to 436.6 million tons. In addition, the 
legal form has changed: whereas the former targets were established 
solely by Presidential Decree, the revised system sets a statutory floor by 
statute, with specific standards detailed in the Presidential Decree. The 
legal meaning of setting a 2030 reduction target has also been redefined. 
Under the former Green Growth Act (see, inter alia, Article 42), it 
served as one of several tools for the efficient and systematic promotion 
- 70 -of low-carbon green growth. Under the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act (see, inter alia, Article 7), it functions as an interim milestone 
toward the national vision of achieving carbon neutrality by 2050. The 
structure of the relevant provisions has likewise undergone major 
revisions. Therefore, even assuming that Article 42, Section (1), Item 1 
of the former Green Growth Act and Article 25, Section (1) of its 2016 
and 2019 former Enforcement Decrees violated the State’s duty to 
protect fundamental rights, as Complainants allege, it is difficult to 
conclude that there remains a risk of recurrence of the same type of 
constitutional violation, or that a determination on the constitutionality of 
the repealed provisions would have implications for a future amendment 
of Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree, such that constitutional 
clarification would carry substantial meaning.
Accordingly, the claim challenging Article 42, Section (1), Item 1 of 
the former Green Growth Act and Article 25, Section (1) of the 2016 
and 2019 former Enforcement Decrees of the same Act is nonjusticiable, 
as Complainants’ cognizable interest has extinguished, and there is no 
need for constitutional clarification.
VI. Analysis of Fiscal Plan at Issue 
A. Whether Budget Constitutes Exercise of Governmental Power 
Although a budget is a type of legal norm established through a 
resolution by the National Assembly, like a statute, it differs from a 
statute in that it binds only State agencies and not the general citizens. 
Even if a budget may affect the fundamental rights of individuals 
through fiscal acts of the government, such effects arise from the 
government’s specific enforcement actions based on relevant statutes and 
regulations, not from the budget itself or the act of the National 
Assembly’s approval thereof. Therefore, a budget passed by the National 
- 71 -Assembly, or the act of approving a budget proposal, does not 
constitute an “exercise of governmental power” under Article 68, Section 
(1) of the Constitutional Court Act and thus cannot be subject to a 
constitutional complaint (see Constitutional Court 2006Hun-Ma409, April 
25, 2006).
B. Assessment 
The Fiscal Plan at Issue pertains to “the scale of and procurement 
methods of financial resources required for the transition to a 
carbon-neutral society and promotion of green growth,” which must be 
included in the National Basic Plan for Carbon Neutrality and Green 
Growth pursuant to Article 10, Section (2), Item 10 of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act. Substantively, it sets forth a mid- to 
long-term investment strategy by the State and provides that “specific 
investment plans may be adjusted in consideration of fiscal conditions, 
project feasibility, etc.” The plan is implemented through the annual 
government budget adopted by the National Assembly.
Thus, by its nature, the Fiscal Plan at Issue is merely a mid- to 
long-term plan concerning the budget, which is drafted by the 
government and approved by the National Assembly, and cannot be 
deemed to constitute an “exercise of governmental power” that directly 
affects the fundamental rights of citizens. Therefore, it does not qualify 
as a subject of a constitutional complaint.
Accordingly, the claim challenging the Fiscal Plan at Issue is 
nonjusticiable.
VII. Analysis of Article 8, Section (1) of Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act, Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree, and Sectoral 
and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue
A. Issue in This Case 
- 72 -1. Fundamental Right in Question Regarding Infringement
(a) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree establish the “mid- to 
long-term national greenhouse gas reduction target” (hereinafter referred 
to as the “mid- to long-term reduction target”), which is to reduce 
national greenhouse gas emissions by 40 percent by 2030 compared to 
the 2018 level. The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are 
administrative plans in which the government has specified detailed 
targets for national greenhouse gas emissions and absorptions by sector 
and by year pursuant to Article 8, Sections (2) and (3) of the same Act 
in order to achieve the aforementioned mid- to long-term reduction 
target.
In particular, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, 
which constitute administrative plans, are “acts of the State that set 
greenhouse gas reduction targets as one of the protective measures in 
response to the dangerous situation posed by the climate crisis.” From 
the perspective of the State’s duty to protect fundamental rights, such 
targets directly affect fundamental rights including the environmental 
right. Given that these targets are expected to be implemented through 
specific and individualized greenhouse gas reduction policies supported 
by the relevant law such as the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, they 
constitute an “exercise of governmental power” subject to a constitutional 
complaint (see, inter alia, Constitutional Court 92Hun-Ma68 etc., 
October 1, 1992; Constitutional Court 2021Hun-Ma271, December 22, 
2022). Furthermore, it is difficult for citizens to challenge targets set by 
law or administrative plans on grounds of their insufficiency through 
other procedures such as administrative litigation (see, inter alia, Seoul 
Administrative Court Judgment 2023GuHap59001, January 25, 2024).
The danger of the climate crisis, which the State seeks to address 
through legal and administrative reduction targets, includes threats caused 
by extreme weather events, water scarcity, food shortages, ocean 
- 73 -acidification, rising sea levels, and ecosystem collapse resulting from 
climate change (see Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, Article 2, Item 
2). These risks encompass potential harm not only to the safety of the 
life and body and health of individuals but also to the natural and living 
environment, in whole or in part. Therefore, the fundamental right most 
directly related to the aforementioned provisions and plans is the 
environmental right. The issue in this case is whether the greenhouse gas 
reduction targets set by the aforementioned provisions and plans infringe 
Complainants’ environmental right.
Complainants argue that the reduction targets imposed by these 
provisions and plans disproportionately shift the burden of reduction 
from the current generation to future generations, thereby infringing 
freedom of future generations in violation of the principle of 
proportionality, and also violate the right to equality. These arguments, 
as will be further addressed below, are premised on the dangerous 
situation of the climate crisis and the characteristics of protective 
measures taken in response. Ultimately, they assert that the State has 
failed to fulfill its obligation under Article 35, Section (1) of the 
Constitution to endeavor to conserve the environment, and thus the 
matter will be addressed under the analysis of whether the environmental 
right has been violated.
(b) Complainants also assert that, in addition to their right to live in a 
healthy and pleasant environment, their general liberty rights—including 
the right to life, right to the pursuit of happiness, right to resist 
extinction, personal liberty, freedoms of residence and movement, 
freedom to choose an occupation, and freedom from intrusion into a 
dwellin g—and their right to a humane life, inter alia, have all been 
infringed.
However, the environmental degradation caused by the climate crisis 
includes risks to life and personal safety, and the right to the pursuit of 
happiness is inherently supplementary to other fundamental rights. The 
- 74 -various additional rights asserted by Complainants are ultimately 
grounded in the premise that environmental degradation caused by the 
climate crisis threatens the foundational conditions for life and thus 
poses a risk of infringing various freedoms and rights. Thus, as this 
Court will determine whether the environmental right, which is most 
closely related to the aforementioned provisions and plans at issue, has 
been infringed, it will not separately assess whether the right to life, the 
right to the pursuit of happiness, and the other rights asserted by 
Complainants have been violated.
2. Standard for Review on Infringement of Environmental Right
(a) Constitutional guarantee of the right to live in a healthy and 
pleasant environment
The Constitution provides that “All citizens shall have the right to live 
in a healthy and pleasant environment, and the State and all citizens 
shall endeavor to conserve the environment” (Article 35, Section (1)), 
thereby guaranteeing the people’s right to a healthy environment while 
also imposing a duty on the State to endeavor to maintain a sound 
environment in which people can live healthily and pleasantly. The 
environmental right serves as a foundation for protecting life and 
personal liberty, and is ultimately a right aimed at securing the “quality 
of life.”
In exercising the environmental right, citizens may assert the right not 
to have their freedom to enjoy a healthy and pleasant environment 
infringed by the State. In certain cases, citizens may also be recognized 
as having the right to demand that the State ensure they can live in such 
an environment. Thus, the environmental right by itself has the nature of 
a comprehensive fundamental right.
Although the content and exercise of the environmental right are to be 
concretely determined by statute (Constitution, Article 35, Section (2)), 
- 75 -the purpose of this constitutional provision is to require the legislature 
to give specific form, through legislation, to the environmental right 
expressly provided for in the Constitution in a manner consistent with its 
intent. This does not mean that the legislature may fail to enact any 
legislation relating to the environmental right, thereby rendering the right 
entirely meaningless, or that it may satisfy the requirement simply by 
enacting any statute regardless of its substance. Therefore, when certain 
conditions are met, if there is no legislation or if the legislation is 
manifestly insufficient to protect the environmental right of the people, 
such that it infringes the environmental right, relief may be sought from 
the Court (see Constitutional Court 2018Hun-Ma730, December 27, 
2019; Constitutional Court 2017Hun-Ma1281, March 26, 2020).
(b) Duty of the State to guarantee the right to live in a healthy and 
pleasant environment
Under Article 10 of the Constitution, the State shall confirm and 
guarantee the inviolable fundamental human rights of individuals. As 
fundamental rights also function as an objective value order of the 
community, the State bears an affirmative duty to protect such rights—at 
least with respect to infringements on essential legal interests, such as 
the protection of life and person —even when those infringements are 
caused by private actors rather than the State.
Given, inter alia, that the State bears an affirmative duty to guarantee 
the fundamental rights of the people; that Article 35, Section (1) of the 
Constitution imposes an obligation on both the State and its citizens to 
endeavor to conserve the environment; that environmental harm is 
frequently caused by private actors, making it necessary for the 
legislature to define the permissible scope thereof; and that 
environmental harm may lead to the infringement of important legal 
interests protected by fundamental rights, such as the protection of life 
and person, the State, in certain cases, has an affirmative duty to take 
protective measures against violations of the environmental right by 
- 76 -private parties (see Constitutional Court 2018Hun-Ma730, December 
27, 2019; Constitutional Court 2017Hun-Ma1281, March 26, 2020).
The scope of the environmental right, which guarantees the “right to 
live in a healthy and pleasant environment,” includes not only the natural 
environment but also the living environment, such as the human-made 
environment (see Constitutional Court 2018Hun-Ma730, December 27, 
2019; Constitutional Court 2017Hun-Ma1281, March 26, 2020).
The duty of the State and its citizens to endeavor to “conserve the 
environment” includes obligations to protect both the natural and living 
environments from pollution and degradation, to improve polluted or 
damaged environments, and to maintain and promote pleasant environmental 
conditions (see Framework Act on Environmental Policy, Article 3, 
Item 6).
Included within this duty is also the obligation to reduce the causes of 
climate change in order to mitigate its effects or to take adaptive 
measures in response to the risks that climate change poses—namely, the 
degradation of the overall environment forming the basis of daily life 
and threats to, inter alia, the safety of life and person.
(c) Principle of prohibition of insufficient protection
1) Although the State bears the duty to protect the people’s right to 
live in a healthy and pleasant environment, the question of how the 
State’s duty to protect fundamental rights should be fulfilled —whether by 
the legislature or by an executive body acting under legislative 
delegation —falls, in principle, within the scope of responsibility of 
the legislature or the executive, in accordance with the principle s 
of separation of powers and democracy. These actors derive their 
democratic legitimacy directly from the people and bear political 
responsibility for their decisions. The Court may review the fulfillment 
of the State’s duty to protect by the legislature or the executive only in 
a limited manner.
- 77 -Therefore, when the Court reviews whether the State has failed to 
fulfill its duty to protect the right to live in a healthy and pleasant 
environment, the applicable standard is whether the State has taken at 
least appropriate and effective minimum protective measures to satisfy 
the so-called “principle of prohibition of insufficient protection” (see, 
inter alia, Constitutional Court 2008Hun-Ma419 etc., December 26, 
2008; Constitutional Court 2012Hun-Ma89 etc., October 21, 2015; 
Constitutional Court 2012Hun-Ma121, October 27, 2016; Constitutional 
Court 2018Hun-Ma730, December 27, 2019; Constitutional Court 
2017Hun-Ma1281, March 26, 2020).
There is no universally applicable or fixed rule to determine when the 
principle of prohibition of insufficient protection is violated. This 
determination must be made by weighing and balancing, in each 
individual case, the type of legal interest at stake, its standing in the 
constitutional order, the nature and extent of the infringement or risk 
involved, the significance of the competing legal interests, etc. (see 
Constitutional Court 2018Hun-Ma730, December 27, 2019).
Whether the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection has been 
violated in a given case depends on whether the “protective measure” 
taken in response to the “dangerous situation” that necessitates protection 
against a foreseeable violation of fundamental rights constitutes the 
minimum necessary measure commensurate with the nature of that risk. 
Where the assessment concerns a matter of technical or scientific 
complexity, or one of an international character, the nature and other 
relevant aspects of the dangerous situation must be reviewed objectively 
on the basis of “scientific facts” and “international standards” (see 
Constitutional Court 2008Hun-Ma419 etc., December 26, 2008; 
Constitutional Court 2012Hun-Ma38, April 30, 2015; Constitutional 
Court 2012Hun-Ma89 etc., October 21, 2015).
2) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree, and the Sectoral and 
- 78 -Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, which set forth the State’s 
greenhouse gas reduction goals, constitute protective measures taken in 
response to a concrete dangerous situation in which climate change 
threatens, inter alia, the lives and personal safety of citizens, including 
Complainants, and undermines the environment that forms the basis of 
their lives, as part of the State’s efforts to reduce greenhouse gas 
emissions, a cause of climate change.
Climate change results from the accumulation of greenhouse gases 
emitted by human activity, causing irreversible changes in Earth’s 
climate system. Addressing climate change requires predictions and 
analyses grounded in specialized scientific knowledge. Moreover, as the 
climate crisis is a common challenge to humanity, international 
cooperation is essential in formulating responsive measures.
The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (hereinafter referred to 
as the “IPCC”), established in November 1988 as a joint body under the 
World Meteorological Organization and the United Nations Environment 
Programme, is an international organization composed of over 3,000 
experts, including meteorologists, oceanographers, glaciologists, and 
economists from around the world, to address global environmental issues 
related to climate change. With 195 member states, including our country, 
the IPCC publishes assessment reports every five to seven years that 
identify trends and drivers of climate change, evaluate its ecological and 
socioeconomic impacts, and analyze response strategies. These reports 
serve as foundational resources for climate change policy, research, and 
international negotiations.
Our country is a party to several multilateral treaties that have entered 
into force through accession or ratification processes, including the 
United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (Treaty No. 
1213, effective March 21, 1994; hereinafter referred to as the 
“UNFCCC”), the Kyoto Protocol to the UNFCCC (Treaty No. 1706, 
effective February 16, 2005; hereinafter the “Kyoto Protocol”), and the 
- 79 -Paris Agreement (Treaty No. 2315, effective December 3, 2016; 
hereinafter the “Paris Agreement”). These treaties codify the international 
community’s concrete efforts to respond to the climate crisis.
3) In this case, the issue is whether Article 8, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree, which set the mid- to long-term reduction target, 
and the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, which set 
targets for emissions and removals by sector and year to achieve those 
goals, violate the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection with 
respect to the State’s duty to protect fundamental rights, including the 
environmental right. In assessing this issue, it is necessary to objectively 
examine the scientific analyses and projections presented in sources such 
as IPCC reports, as well as the standards for international action 
established under agreements such as the Paris Agreement, with respect 
to the nature of the climate crisis as a dangerous situation and the nature 
of the State’s greenhouse gas reduction measures that are required in 
response.
(d) Principle of statutory reservation
1) The Constitution upholds the rule of law as one of its fundamental 
principles, and the principle of statutory reservatio n—namely, that 
administrative actions require a basis in formal legislation enacted by the 
National Assembly —forms a core component of that rule. Furthermore, 
the principle of statutory reservation today is understood to encompass 
not merely the requirement that administrative actions be based on 
statute, but also the requiremen t—embodied in the principle of 
parliamentary reservatio n—that, in areas of fundamental and significant 
importance to the national community and its members, particularly those 
related to the realization of fundamental rights of the citizens, essential 
matters must be decided not by the administration but directly by the 
legislature, as the representative of the people. What matters must be 
governed by formal legislation enacted by the legislature itself cannot be 
- 80 -uniformly or categorically defined. Rather, such determination must be 
made on a case-by-case basis, taking into account, inter alia, the 
importance of the interests or values involved and the degree and 
manner of regulation or infringement. However, at the very least, where 
a constitutionally guaranteed freedom or right of the people is to be 
restricted, the essential aspects of such restriction must be governed by 
statute enacted by the legislature itself (see, inter alia, Constitutional 
Court 98Hun-Ba70, May 27, 1999).
The legislative process of the National Assembly is a process wherein 
important decisions of the community are made through open debate 
among a pluralistic body composed of the people’s representatives. It 
enables recognition and reconciliation of diverse perspectives and 
interests within society, and allows for criticism and participation by the 
general citizens and opposition parties. This stands in contrast to 
administrative rule-making, which is carried out solely by professional 
bureaucrats, and makes the legislative process more appropriate for the 
discovery of the public interest and the fair adjustment of competing 
interests. From this perspective, the greater the significance of the matter 
being regulated in relation to fundamental rights—and the more necessary 
open discussion and balancing of conflicting interests becomes —the 
greater the necessity for direct statutory regulation by the National 
Assembly, and the higher the required level of regulatory specificity 
(see, inter alia, Constitutional Court 2001Hun-Ma882, March 25, 2004; 
Constitutional Court 2007Hun-Ba63, October 29, 2009).
2) Article 37, Section (2) of the Constitution sets forth the general 
principle of statutory reservation concerning restrictions on fundamental 
rights, and Article 35, Section (2) of the Constitution provides that “The 
matters concerning the substance and exercise of the environmental right 
shall be determined by statute.” Thus, the extent to which the specific 
content and level of protective measures for the environmental right must 
be prescribed by statute may give rise to an issue of statutory reservation 
or parliamentary reservation. Therefore, in determining whethe r 
- 81 -Complainants’ environmental right has been infringed, the standard of 
review must also consider whether there has been a violation of the 
principle of statutory reservatio n—including the issue of parliamentary 
reservatio n—within the scope of the State’s duty to protect fundamental 
rights.
3) Complainants also assert that Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement 
Decree violate the principle against blanket delegation. However, this 
argument essentially contends that essential matters regarding greenhouse 
gas reduction target s—such as the setting of reduction pathway s—were 
not prescribed directly by statute but delegated to subordinate legislation, 
and is therefore not distinguishable from a claim of parliamentary 
reservation violation.
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
delegates to Presidential Decree only the specific numerical percentage of 
the 2030 reduction target, within the range of at least 35 percent 
compared to the national greenhouse gas emissions in 2018. It does not 
delegate the regulation of reduction pathways for the periods from 2018 
to 2030 and from 2031 to 2049. Insofar as the delegation to Presidential 
Decree is concerned, Complainants argue that setting the minimum 
reduction at 35 percent of the 2018 level is insufficient. However, this 
pertains to whether the provision violates the principle of prohibition of 
insufficient protection. Therefore, the issue of a violation of the principle 
against blanket delegation is not separately examined in this case.
Likewise, the claim that Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement 
Decre e—enacted pursuant to the delegation in Article 8, Section (1) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act—violates the principle of statutory 
reservation or the principle of prohibition of blanket delegation by setting 
the reduction target at 40 percent is, in substance, indistinguishable from 
the claim of violation of the prohibition of insufficient protection, and is 
therefore not separately assessed.
- 82 -3.Dangerous Situation, Nature of Protective Measures, and Standard 
of Review in This Case
(a) Climate crisis as a dangerous situation
1) It is a scientific fact, repeatedly highlighted in reports of the IPCC 
and others, that the emission of greenhouse gases caused by human 
activities is a major factor driving global warming. 
Greenhouse gases emitted through activities such as the use of fossil 
fuels by humans are not fully absorbed by the natural climate system, 
and the residual portion continues to accumulate in the atmosphere, 
resulting in a persistent increase in the total atmospheric amount of 
greenhouse gases. The higher the concentration of greenhouse gases, the 
greater the temperature increase on Earth due to the greenhouse effect. 
According to the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report, whose Synthesis 
Report’s Summary for Policymakers was approved on March 19, 2023, 
the global surface temperature during the period 2011–2020 was 1.09°C 
higher than in the pre-industrial period of 1850–1900, and for every 
1,000 gigatons CO₂ (GtCO ₂) emitted by human activity, the global 
surface temperature rises by 0.45°C.
Such global warming leads to a transformation of the climate system 
that goes beyond the range of natural variability and does not return to 
an average state. Climate change in turn triggers extreme weather events
—such as heatwaves, droughts, and floods —water shortages, food 
insecurity, ocean acidification, rising sea levels, and the collapse of 
ecosystems around the globe. These negative impacts of climate change, 
which threaten human life and health and damage the natural 
environment and the living environment based thereon, become more 
severe and widespread as global warming intensifies.
In particular, if the global temperature rise reaches a certain tipping 
point, there is a probability that climate change may occur in sudden and 
irreversible ways. For instance, sea level rise worldwide could reach 
- 83 -several meters or more due to large-scale melting of ice sheets, or 
circulation patterns of ocean currents in certain regions may change, 
causing drastic shifts in climate in those affected areas.
Actions to suppress the negative effects of climate change must 
include both “mitigation,” which aims to reduce the emission of 
greenhouse gases as the main cause of global warming, and “adaptation,” 
which encompasses various efforts to reduce or prevent the damage 
based on the premise of climate change. However, the more global 
warming intensifies and its resulting damage increases, the more difficult 
and costly adaptation measures become. Therefore, efforts to reduce the 
amount of greenhouse gases emitted by human activity are essential in 
order to slow the pace of global warming, which is a cause of the 
climate crisis.
2) The effects of greenhouse gas emissions on the climate system, the 
risks of climate change resulting therefrom, the impact of emission 
reduction efforts, inter alia, are all global in nature. Efforts to reduce 
greenhouse gas emissions are governed by standards for action 
established through international agreements such as treaties. 
In the case of our country, rises in average temperature, sea surface 
temperature, and sea level have been observed. Summers have become 
longer, winters shorter, and extreme weather events —such as typhoons, 
heatwaves, and heavy snow—are occurring with increasing frequency, all 
reflecting the global impact of climate change. According to, inter alia, 
the “2021 Evaluation of Greenhouse Gas Reduction Implementation 
Performance” (December 2022) by the Greenhouse Gas Inventory and 
Research Center of Korea, our country’s greenhouse gas emissions 
continued to rise until 2018, and its per capita greenhouse gas emissions 
in 2021 reached approximately 13.1 tons. Therefore, our country cannot 
be free from responsibility for climate change caused by greenhouse gas 
emissions. 
Multilateral treaties constituting the so-called UN Climate Regime, 
- 84 -such as the UNFCCC, the Kyoto Protocol, and the Paris Agreement, 
are international norms concerning climate change that have entered into 
force in our country through the processes of accession and ratification.
3) Because the increase in global average temperature is scientifically 
proven to correlate with the level of greenhouse gases accumulated in 
the atmosphere due to human activities, the impacts of climate change 
caused by greenhouse gas emissions are characterized by “irreversibility”
—they cannot be undone once emissions have occurred. As a result, the 
later efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions are undertaken, the 
greater the risks posed by climate change and the heavier the burden of 
adaptation measures becomes. Thus, there is an “urgent necessity” for 
reducing greenhouse gases. In order to limit the rise in global average 
temperature to a specific level, emissions must be reduced to a point 
where they are balanced with absorption, such that greenhouse gases no 
longer accumulate in the atmosphere.
When the State sets greenhouse gas reduction targets and seeks to 
implement them, such actions function, on the one hand, as protective 
measures to conserve the environment in response to the threat of the 
climate crisis. On the other hand, they may also restrict or alter 
economic activities or lifestyles that emit greenhouse gases, such as the 
use of fossil fuels. Furthermore, as these measures may require structural 
changes across the national industries and people’s daily lives—including 
various and extensive restrictions related to land use, development, and 
conservatio n—they also restrict individual liberties, such as the freedom 
of occupation and the exercise of property rights. Since the level of 
increase in the global average temperature is proportional not to 
emissions at a single point in time but to the cumulative amount in the 
atmosphere, delayed reduction efforts can intensify the burdens of 
restricting greenhouse gas-emitting economic activities and lifestyles. 
This also constitutes an issue of conserving the living environment.
(b)Establishing greenhouse gas reduction targets as protective measures
- 85 -1) To prevent the severe impacts of the climate crisis, both mitigation 
of or delay in climate change through greenhouse gas reductions and 
adaptation measures to minimize the effects and repercussions of the 
crisis are necessary (see Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, Article 1, 
Article 2, Items 7, 11). As global warming intensifies, adaptation 
becomes increasingly difficult, with more areas reaching the limits of 
adaptability. For this reason, the internationally agreed temperature goal 
to limit the rise in global average temperature carries significant weight 
in setting greenhouse gas reduction targets as a protective measure in 
response to the climate crisis.
Article 2, paragraph 1(a) of the Paris Agreement states as the objective 
of the Agreement the goal of “Holding the increase in the global average 
temperature to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels and pursuing 
efforts to limit the temperature increase to 1.5°C above pre-industrial 
levels, recognizing that this would significantly reduce the risks and 
impacts of climate change,” thereby setting a global temperature goal 
aimed at halting the progression of global warming.
Article 4, paragraph 1 of the Paris Agreement prescribes that “In order 
to achieve the long-term temperature goal set out in Article 2, Parties 
aim to reach global peaking of greenhouse gas emissions as soon as 
possible, recognizing that peaking will take longer for developing 
country Parties, and to undertake rapid reductions thereafter in 
accordance with best available science, so as to achieve a balance 
between anthropogenic emissions by sources and removals by sinks of 
greenhouse gases in the second half of this century, on the basis of 
equity, and in the context of sustainable development and efforts to 
eradicate poverty.”
Article 3, Item 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act establishes 
as one of the basic principles “the active participation in the efforts of 
the international community to hold the rise in the global average 
temperature to no more than 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial 
- 86 -levels, based on the recognition that the climate crisis is a common 
challenge for humanity.” Article 2, Item 3 of the same Act defines 
“carbon neutrality” as a “state in which net emissions, obtained by 
offsetting the amount of greenhouse gases absorbed from the amount of 
greenhouse gases emitted, discharged, or leaked into the atmosphere, are 
zero.” Item 4 of the same Article defines a “carbon-neutral society” as a 
“society in which people lower or eliminate dependence on fossil fuels 
and lay the foundation for finance, technology, system, etc. for 
adaptation to the climate crisis and a just transition to smoothly achieve 
carbon neutrality and prevent and minimize damage and adverse effects 
that may arise in the process.” Based on the above, Article 7, Section 
(1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act provides that “The 
government shall establish a national vision for transition to a 
carbon-neutral society and promotion of harmonious development of 
economy and environment, with the aim of achieving carbon neutrality 
by 2050.”
Article 8, Section (1) of the same Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree adopt the principle of participating in the 
international efforts to limit the increase in global average temperature to 
no more than 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, as set forth 
in the Paris Agreement. They are premised on the national goal of 
“carbon neutrality by 2050” under Article 7, Section (1) of the Act, 
which aims to achieve a balance, in the latter half of this century, 
between anthropogenic emissions from emission sources and removals by 
sinks. Based on this, our country has set a mid- to long-term reduction 
target for 2030 on the assumption of a continuous reduction of 
greenhouse gas emissions from the peak level in 2018 through the target 
year of 2050.
2) Climate change is a global issue, both in its causes and its impacts. 
No country can claim to be entirely free of responsibility for 
contributing to the climate crisis, and thus cannot avoid its share of 
responsibility merely by pointing to other countries’ emissions.
- 87 -At the same time, climate change has discriminatory impacts that vary 
by, inter alia, nation, region, class, age, and gender. While developed 
countries bear greater responsibility for greenhouse gas emissions, the 
damage from climate change is more severe in developing countries, 
which have limited adaptive capacity. The scale and nature of the 
damage also vary depending on the geographical conditions of each 
country. Moreover, developing countries are more vulnerable than 
developed countries in terms of their technological, economic, and 
industrial structural capacity to reduce emissions, and geographic 
conditions affecting reduction capacity differ by country.
The Paris Agreement establishes a system in which both developed 
and developing countries jointly participate in efforts to reduce 
greenhouse gas emissions, without distinguishing between them. With 
respect to each country’s responsibilities for emissions reduction, Article 
2, paragraph 2 provides that implementation shall “reflect equity and the 
principle of common but differentiated responsibilities and respective 
capabilities, in light of different national circumstances.” As a means of 
doing so, Articles 3 and 4 of the Paris Agreement require each Party to 
prepare, communicate, maintain, and implement its nationally determined 
contribution by itself.
Specifically, Article 4 of the Paris Agreement provides in paragraph 2 
that “Each Party shall prepare, communicate and maintain successive 
nationally determined contributions that it intends to achieve. Parties 
shall pursue domestic mitigation measures, with the aim of achieving the 
objectives of such contributions.” Paragraph 3 of the same Article states 
that “Each Party’s successive nationally determined contribution will 
represent a progression beyond the Party’s then current nationally 
determined contribution and reflect its highest possible ambition, 
reflecting its common but differentiated responsibilities and respective 
capabilities, in the light of different national circumstances.” Paragraph 9 
of the same Article provides that “Each Party shall communicate a 
nationally determined contribution every five years in accordance with 
- 88 -decision 1/CP.21 and any relevant decisions of the Conference of the 
Parties serving as the meeting of the Parties to this Agreement, and be 
informed by the outcomes of the global stocktake referred to in Article 
14.” In addition, Article 14 of the Paris Agreement provides that the 
Conference of the Parties serving as the meeting of the Parties to this 
Agreement shall periodically take stock of the implementation of this 
Agreement to assess the collective progress toward achieving the purpose 
of this Agreement and its long-term goals, undertaking a global stocktake 
every five years starting in 2023.
The mid- to long-term reduction target set in Article 8, Section (1) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decre e—namely, reducing national greenhouse gas emissions 
by 40 percent from the 2018 levels by 2030—constitutes a core element 
of our country’s nationally determined contribution, which was submitted 
and registered with the UNFCCC Secretariat in December 2021 in 
accordance with the standards of the Paris Agreement.
The Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue were established by the 
government pursuant to Article 8, Section (2) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act to achieve the above mid- to long-term reduction target 
by 2030, setting forth “sectoral greenhouse gas reduction goals” 
(hereinafter referred to as “sectoral reduction goals”), including industry, 
buildings, transportation, power generation, and waste. The Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue were established by the government pursuant 
to Article 8, Section (3) of the same Act, with the aim of achieving both 
the mid- to long-term reduction target and the Sectoral Reduction Targets 
at Issue, setting forth yearly greenhouse gas reduction goals for the State 
as a whole and for each sector (hereinafter referred to as “yearly 
reduction goals”). Thus, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue constitute “domestic mitigation measures” pursued to achieve the 
objective of our country’s nationally determined contributions, as 
provided under Article 4, paragraph 2 of the Paris Agreement.
- 89 -Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
provides: “The government shall re-examine mid- to long-term reduction 
targets, sectoral reduction targets, and annual reduction targets 
(hereinafter referred to as ‘mid- to long-term reduction target, etc.’) 
every five years in consideration of domestic and international 
conditions, such as the Paris Agreement (hereinafter referred to as the 
‘Agreement’), and shall modify or reset such targets in accordance with 
the principle of progression under Article 4 of the Agreement, if 
necessary: Provided , That if it is necessary due to changes in social and 
technical conditions, etc. the targets may be modified or reset before five 
years elapse.” This establishes a normative framework for continuously 
enhancing efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, in accordance with 
the principle set forth in Article 4, paragraph 3 of the Paris Agreemen t—
that each successive nationally determined contribution will represent a 
progression beyond the Party’s then current nationally determined 
contribution (hereinafter referred to as the “principle of progression” )—
while complying with the procedures of the global stocktake under 
Article 4, paragraph 9 and Article 14 of the Paris Agreement.
(c) Standard of review in this case
1) The Constitution not only provides in Article 35, Section (1) that 
“the State and all citizens shall endeavor to conserve the environment,” 
but also states in Article 34, Section (6) that “the State shall endeavor to 
prevent disasters and to protect citizens from harm therefrom.” The harm 
to life, person, and property, inter alia, caused by phenomena such as 
extreme weather, water scarcity, food shortage, ocean acidification, sea 
level rise, and ecosystem collaps e—all resulting from climate chang e—
may be characterized as disasters or calamities. The State’s formulation 
and implementation of greenhouse gas reduction goals and plans in 
response to the climate crisis constitutes not only an effort to conserve 
the environment under Article 35, Section (1) of the Constitution, but 
also an effort to prevent disasters under Article 34, Section (6) of the 
- 90 -Constitution. These efforts fall within the scope of the State’s 
constitutional obligations.
Environmental rights are so-called third-generation human rights and 
require international cooperation for their full realization. The climate 
crisis is a domain that calls for global solidarity. If a country neglects its 
greenhouse gas reduction efforts on the grounds that it cannot effectively 
respond to the global climate crisis through its own efforts alone, or 
because other countries’ efforts are insufficient, then, in the end, all 
countries’ reduction efforts will fall short, global action against the 
climate crisis will fail, and environmental rights will not be effectively 
guaranteed. Conversely, if each country makes its best efforts to reduce 
emissions, such efforts will mutually reinforce one another, discourage 
free-riding, and enable an effective global response to the climate crisis 
that can ensure the meaningful protection of environmental rights.
Thus, the interpretation of constitutional environmental rights may take 
international standards into account. In this context, when setting 
greenhouse gas reduction targets as part of its response to the climate 
crisis, the State must ensure that our country’s contribution is 
commensurate with its share in the global reduction effort. 
2) Article 35, Section (1) of the Constitution places the duty of 
“environmental conservation” not only on the State but also on all 
citizens. Article 122 of the Constitution provides that the State may, as 
prescribed by statute, impose restrictions and obligations necessary for 
the efficient, balanced utilization and development, and “conservation” of 
“national territory, which constitutes the basis for the productive 
activities and daily lives of all citizens.” Environmental “conservation” 
under Article 35, Section (1) includes maintaining environmental 
conditions essential for production and livelihood in the future without 
degradation.
In particular, it is a well-established scientific fact that the primary 
driver of climate chang e—the rise in global average temperatur e—is 
- 91 -almost directly proportional to the accumulation of greenhouse gases in 
the atmosphere. Therefore, inadequate emission reduction efforts today 
exacerbate future burdens, both in terms of exposure to the adverse 
effects of climate change and in terms of the economic and lifestyle 
constraints necessary to mitigate it. This is a defining feature of the 
climate crisis.
The preamble to the Constitution affirms the determination “to ensure 
security, liberty and happiness for ourselves and our posterity forever.” 
Thus, when the State designs protective measures to respond to the 
climate crisis, it must ensure that excessive burdens are not transferred to 
future generations. This is necessary not only to safeguard the freedom 
of future citizens but also to uphold equality in the guarantee of 
fundamental rights across present and future generations. Article 3, Item 
1 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act reflects this principle by 
stating that the transition to a carbon-neutral society and the pursuit of 
green growth “shall be based on the principle of intergenerational equity 
that the current generation is responsible for ensuring the survival of 
future generations and the principle of sustainable development.”
Therefore, when the State sets and implements greenhouse gas 
reduction targets as a response to the climate crisis, it is constitutionally 
demanded to take into account responsibility for future environmental 
conditions. Considering that emission reductions must continue until a 
balance is achieved between emissions and removals, the State should 
establish its targets and plans in such a way that excessive burdens are 
not shifted to the future, whether in terms of climate impacts or 
emission constraints.
3) The setting of greenhouse gas reduction targets by the State as a 
mitigation measure in response to the climate crisis aims to reduce the 
amount of greenhouse gases emitted and accumulated in the atmosphere 
by encouraging efforts across all sectors of production and daily life by 
both the State and its citizens. For this reason, the system for setting 
- 92 -such reduction targets must be institutionalized in a manner that 
ensures the actual realization of emission reductions.
In this regard, several issues may arise, including whether a mid- to 
long-term timeline for quantitative reduction targets exists, whether there 
are rules governing failures to meet post-reduction emission levels, and 
whether the legal framework regulating climate mitigation measures is 
effective.
4) Whether the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue and 
the mid- to long-term reduction target set by Article 8, Section (1) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree violate the principle of prohibition of insufficient 
protection must be assessed based on whether they meet the minimum 
requirements necessary as protective measures commensurate with the 
nature of the climate crisis as a dangerous situation.
As discussed above, this requires evaluating whether our country’s 
specific greenhouse gas reduction targets align with its fair share in the 
global reduction effort; whether the institutional framework for setting 
such targets ensures that excessive burdens are not transferred to the 
future, in terms of climate impacts and emission limitations; and whether 
the system effectively guarantees emission reductions. Such assessment 
must take into account scientific facts and international standards.
In addition, whether Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act violates the principle of statutory reservation, including 
the parliamentary reservation principle, must be assessed in light of the 
nature that the mechanism for setting greenhouse gas reduction targets, 
being a matter governed by this provision, must possess as a protective 
measure against the climate crisis.
B.Assessment of Article 8, Section (1) of Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree
- 93 -1. Summary of Issues
(a) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
prescribes that, regarding the mid- to long-term reduction target, the 
national greenhouse gas emissions are to be reduced by a ratio 
prescribed by Presidential Decree within the range of not less than 35 
percent from the 2018 level by 2030. Article 3, Section (1) of the 
Enforcement Decree of the same Act sets this ratio at 40 percent.
With respect to the alleged violation of the prohibition of insufficient 
protection, the issue arises as to whether the 40 percent reduction target 
by 2030 is consistent with our country’s fair share of the global 
greenhouse gas reduction effort and whether it unduly shifts excessive 
burdens to the future in terms of the impacts of climate change and the 
constraints on greenhouse gas emissions.
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act sets 
only the reduction ratio by 2030 relative to the 2018 level, without 
specifying any quantified reduction targets for the period from 2031 to 
2049—the years leading up to the carbon neutrality target year of 2050. 
Instead, Section (4) of the same Article merely provides a framework for 
revising targets every five years in accordance with the principle of 
progression. A question arises as to whether this regulatory scheme shifts 
excessive burdens to the future period from 2031 onward.
Complainants argue that Article 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act lacks effective mechanisms to ensure the implementation of the 
reduction targets. They assert that the regulatory framework for setting 
greenhouse gas reduction targets does not contain any or sufficient 
provisions to incorporate shortfalls into future targets when specific 
quantitative goals are not met. In this connection, the issue arises as to 
whether the mechanism for setting greenhouse gas reduction targets 
under Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act is 
designed to ensure the effective reduction of greenhouse gases.
- 94 -(b) As for the alleged violation of the principle of statutory 
reservation, the issue arises from the fact that, regarding the reduction 
target for 2030, the statute sets only the minimum percentage compared 
to the 2018 level by the target year of 2030, while delegating the 
specific percentage to be prescribed by Presidential Decree. The 
reduction pathway is to follow the sectoral and yearly reduction goals 
established by the government under Article 8, Sections (2) and (3) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act.
Additionally, with respect to the reduction targets for the period from 
2031 to 2049, the statute does not prescribe any quantitative levels but 
delegates this matter to the government through the five-year update 
mechanism under Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act.
2. Whether Prohibition of Insufficient Protection Has Been Violated
(a) Reduction target by 2030
1) Based on the scientific finding that the global average temperature 
rises in proportion to the accumulated concentration of greenhouse gases 
in the atmosphere, the remaining global carbon budge t—i.e., the total 
allowable emissions after a specific year—can be estimated to keep 
temperature rise within a certain target threshold. According to the 
IPCC’s Sixth Assessment Report, the best estimates of the remaining 
carbon budgets from the beginning of 2020 are 500 GtCO 2 for a 50% 
likelihood of limiting global warming to 1.5°C and 1150 GtCO 2 for a 
67% likelihood of limiting warming to 2°C. 
Based on scientific findings in IPCC reports and other sources, 
international consensus on the direction, standards, etc. for global action 
in response to the climate crisis may be formed through multilateral 
treaties such as the UNFCCC and the Paris Agreement, and through 
their implementation mechanisms, including the Conferences of the 
- 95 -Parties. Paragraph 27 of Decision 1/CMA.5 on the Global Stocktake, 
adopted at the 28th session of the Conference of the Parties to the 
UNFCCC in 2023, states: “Also recognizes that limiting global warming 
to 1.5°C with no or limited overshoot requires deep, rapid and sustained 
reductions in global greenhouse gas emissions of 43 per cent by 2030 
and 60 per cent by 2035 relative to the 2019 level and reaching net zero 
carbon dioxide emissions by 2050.”
Premised on the scientifically estimated global carbon budget and the 
shared understanding of global greenhouse gas reduction pathways 
formed through international agreements grounded in scientific research, 
each country must determine its fair share of the global reduction effort 
by setting its own greenhouse gas reduction targets and must formulate 
and implement the necessary policies to achieve those targets.
In this regard, Complainants argue that our country’s 2030 mid- to 
long-term national greenhouse gas reduction target of a 40 percent 
reduction compared to the 2018 level falls significantly short of our 
country’s fair share of the global reduction effort, considering the 
Paris Agreement “principle of equity and common but differentiated 
responsibilities and respective capabilities, in the light of different 
national circumstances.”
Complainants further assert that our country’s carbon budget can be 
fairly and reasonably calculated based on our country’s population ratio 
relative to the global population, and that, when comparing this budget 
with the 2030 reduction targets set by Article 8, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree and with the annual emission targets under the 
Plans at Issue, our country will exhaust its 1.5°C carbon budget by 
around 2025 and its 1.7°C carbon budget by around 2028. It is also 
asserted that even when our country’s carbon budget is calculated 
according to its current share of global emissions, the carbon budget 
consistent with the 1.5°C target will be exhausted around 2032, and the 
- 96 -budget consistent with the 1.7°C target will be depleted around 2042.
Moreover, Complainants claim that our country, based on IPCC 
criteria, qualifies as a developed country in all indicators related to 
“capacity” for responding to the climate crisis, and ranks among the top 
countries in terms of current emissions, per capita emissions, and 
cumulative emissions, which relate to “responsibility.” Therefore, they 
argue that although our country should make a greater contribution to 
the global reduction effort, the 40 percent reduction target by 2030 does 
not meet the globally recognized reduction rate of 43 percent from 2019.
Complainants argue that our country’s 2030 greenhouse gas reduction 
target ratio, whether based on 2010—the year our country began setting 
reduction target s—or on 2018, the peak year of emissions, is lower than 
that of major countries when comparing the reduction rate of net 
emissions, calculated by subtracting absorption and removal amounts 
from gross emissions.
The United Nations Environment Programme (hereinafter referred to as 
the “UNEP”) annually publishes the “Emissions Gap Report,” which 
analyzes the current state of greenhouse gas reduction efforts, including 
the “emissions gap”—the difference between the emissions reductions 
pledged by each country and the reductions needed to achieve the goals 
of the Paris Agreement from a global perspective. According to the 2023 
Emissions Gap Report, even if all nationally determined contributions are 
fully implemented, the global average temperature is projected to rise by 
2.9 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels. Complainants argue that, 
in order to help close this emissions gap, the 2030 reduction target in 
our country’s nationally determined contribution must also be raised.
However, countries differ in various respects such as population, gross 
domestic product, historical levels of greenhouse gas emissions, 
technological and economic capacity to reduce emissions, and industrial 
structure. Although numerous studies have examined which factors 
should be taken into account and how they should be considered to 
- 97 -ensure a fair distribution of emissions reduction efforts among 
countries, there is no agreed-upon standar d—applicable to our country 
and unlikely to be challenged internationall y—that sets concrete criteria 
for the specific burden each country should bear.
When a country sets a “specific numerical target” for the greenhouse 
gas reduction ratio for a “particular year,” various factors may be taken 
into accoun t—not only the method of allocating the carbon budget and 
comprehensive indicators relating to the status of developed and 
developing countries, but also additional elements such as industrial 
structure, geographic conditions, and energy supply circumstances. In the 
present case as well, the government points to difficulties in achieving 
rapid reductions due to an industrial structure centered on manufacturing, 
the relatively late peak in emissions compared to other developed 
countries, and the need for a stable energy supply.
Under the Paris Agreement, preparing and communicating nationally 
determined contributions and providing relevant information fall within 
the government’s diplomatic authority. In exercising this authority, the 
government may take into account factors such as energy security —
including the nation’s capacity to respond to energy crise s—and the 
principle that each successive nationally determined contribution must 
represent a progression beyond the previous one and must not be 
regressive.
In the absence of clear criteria for a fair distribution of greenhouse gas 
reduction targets among countries, it is also unclear whether the global 
emissions gap at a specific point in time can be directly interpreted as 
the emissions gap of individual countries.
Article 4, paragraph 1 of the Paris Agreement requires Parties to “aim 
to undertake rapid reductions in accordance with best available science,” 
and paragraph 3 provides that each Party’s nationally determined 
contribution “will reflect its highest possible ambition.” In principle, our 
country must establish and implement its nationally determine d 
- 98 -contribution in accordance with these principles. However, the 
authority and responsibility to determine which factors should be 
considered and how and to set the specific numerical target at a given 
point in time lie in principle with the legislature and an executive body 
to whom such authority is delegated.
When the numerical value of a greenhouse gas reduction target ratio is 
derived using various statistical evaluation techniques, formulas, and 
step-by-step assessment factors that inherently involve significant 
uncertainty and estimation, there is currently no internationally accepted 
consensus or publicly disclosed calculation procedure by a reliable 
domestic institution that could preclude international objections or be 
invoked by judicial bodies. Moreover, determining the specific numerical 
value of a greenhouse gas reduction target ratio inevitably involves 
consideration of broader factors, such as socioeconomic and foreign 
policy concerns.
Therefore, when the legislature or an executive body delegated by it 
has taken such various factors into account and determined a “specific 
numerical target” for a “particular year,” it is, in principle, difficult for 
the Court to apply the principle of prohibition of insufficient protectio n—
premised on the legislature’s or the delegated executive body’s authority
