and responsibilit y—and to reach the conclusion that such a target does 
not correspond to our country’s fair share in global greenhouse gas 
reduction efforts merely because it does not align with the results 
produced by certain estimation methods or evaluative elements.
2) The mid- to long-term reduction target of reducing the nation’s 
greenhouse gas emissions by 40% by 2030 compared to 2018 was 
established on the premise of continuous greenhouse gas reductions from 
2018, the year when national emissions peaked, through 2050, the target 
year for carbon neutrality, with 2030 serving as an interim milestone.
If greenhouse gas reductions follow a linear pathway from the 2018 
national emission level to net zero emissions by 2050, the corresponding 
- 99 -emission level for 2030 would represent approximately a 37.5% 
reduction compared to 2018. When setting specific reduction targets, 
various factors must be taken into account, including impacts on 
different industries and citizens’ daily lives, the pace of technological 
advancements such as renewable energy, and the feasibility of 
implementation based on sector-specific reduction measures. In this 
regard, Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
sets 35% as the minimum reduction target and directs that a higher 
reduction rate be established by Presidential Decree. Pursuant to this 
delegation, Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree of the same 
Act designates the 40% reduction rate—which exceeds the linear 
reduction pathwa y—as the target reduction ratio.
Complainants argue that such a reduction target, as described above, 
prematurely exhausts our country’s carbon budget, and that the linear 
reduction pathway has no legal significance. Moreover, they contend that 
the pathway represents not an “equal rate” but an “equal amount” of 
reduction, thereby increasing the actual reduction burden in the latter 
period and shifting an excessive burden into the future.
However, with respect to the allocation of our country’s carbon 
budget, there is no internationally agreed-upon or nationally certifiable 
objective methodology in place. The shape of the reduction pathway is 
influenced by various factors, such as the characteristics of individual 
reduction measures and the combinations thereof. The 2030 reduction 
target ratio was, on its face, set at a level that exceeds the linear 
reduction pathway as an interim milestone toward achieving carbon 
neutrality by 2050. However, this cannot be viewed as having 
determined the overall shape of the entire reduction pathway.
In addition, according to the materials submitted in this case—such 
as the “Proposal for an Enhanced 2030 Nationally Determine d 
Contribution” adopted by the government on October 18, 2021—the 
target of reducing emissions by 40% by 2030 compared to 2018, as set 
- 100 -forth in Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree pursuant to 
the delegation under Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act, was determined through procedures that considered 
various sectoral reduction strategies and target levels and incorporated 
diverse opinions. Thus, it is difficult to view this target as having been 
set solely on the premise of a linear reduction pathway.
It is clear that the 2030 reduction target ratio serves as an interim goal 
premised on gradual and continuous reductions from 2018, the year of 
peak emissions, to the target year of carbon neutrality in 2050. Given 
that the determination of the specific numerical value of the target is 
influenced by a variety of considerations and variable s—including the 
characteristics of individual reduction measures and their combination s—
it is difficult to conclude that the target shifts an excessive burden to the 
future solely based on that numerical value.
3) Therefore, the numerical value of the 2030 greenhouse gas 
reduction target set under Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree 
cannot, in itself, be deemed to lack the minimum characteristics required 
of a protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of 
the climate crisis.
(b) Reduction targets from 2031 to 2049
1) The conclusion that the numerical value of the 2030 greenhouse gas 
reduction targe t—namely, a 40% reduction compared to 2018—cannot, 
by itself, be regarded as lacking the minimum characteristics required of 
a protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of the 
climate crisis merely means that it is difficult to declare the figure 
unconstitutional based on a specific methodology over which differing 
opinions may exist concerning the appropriate quantitative target for a 
given year. This does not imply that the figure represents the optimal 
interim target for achieving carbon neutrality by 2050.
- 101 -The 2030 reduction target is based on the assumption that national 
greenhouse gas emissions peaked in 2018. However, it is an undeniable 
fact that, despite our country having established greenhouse gas emission 
reduction targets and implemented relevant legal frameworks since 
around 2009, emissions continued to rise through approximately 2018. In 
this regard, even if the target set for 2030 based on 2018 emissions 
cannot be considered scientifically or policy-wise optimal, it may be 
understood as reflecting the increased burden resulting from the 
continuous rise in emissions up to 2018—including the need to improve 
relevant conditions such as an emission-intensive industrial structure.
In light of this, if all necessary and feasible measures are not taken in 
advance to achieve the current 2030 reduction target and to strengthen 
the reduction targets for 2031 and beyond, the reduction burden for 2031 
and beyond will inevitably increase, making it more difficult to establish 
and implement scientifically and policy-wise sufficient reduction targets. 
Ultimately, this will heighten the risk of failing to achieve the 2050 
carbon neutrality goal.
Thus, the framework for setting the country’s mid- to long-term 
greenhouse gas reduction targets must be designed in a manner that 
ensures actual, gradual reductions up to the 2050 carbon neutrality target 
year, so as to avoid shifting an excessive burden to the future. The 
scrutiny of the institutional effectiveness of this framework should be 
stricter, given that the later occurrence of the emissions peak increases 
the need for rapid reductions.
In light of Article 4, paragraph 1 of the Paris Agreement, which 
addresses the overall trajectory of emission increases, reductions, and 
achieving carbon neutrality, our country is currently at the stage aiming 
to implement rapid reductions based on the best available science 
following the attainment of peak emissions. Therefore, the aforementioned 
scrutiny of institutional effectiveness aligns with international standards of 
action.
- 102 -2) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act only 
sets forth the reduction target ratio for the mid- to long-term target up to 
2030. It does not provide any form of quantitative standard for reduction 
targets from 2031 until 2049, the year prior to the 2050 carbon 
neutrality target year.
Although Article 7, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act establishes the 2050 carbon neutrality goal, and Article 8, Section 
(4) of the same Act requires the government, in principle, to modify or 
reset greenhouse gas reduction targets every five years in accordance 
with the principle of progression, this cannot be considered an effective 
mechanism to prevent the shifting of an excessive burden to the future, 
for the reasons set out below.
a) The requirement in Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act to reexamine, modify, or reset greenhouse gas reduction 
targets every five years is aligned with the communication cycle for 
nationally determined contributions under Article 4, paragraph 9 of the 
Paris Agreement. The fact that the target timeframe for nationally 
determined contributions is “ten years from the communication year” is 
not explicitly provided for in either the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
or the Paris Agreement. Rather, it is based on decision 6/CMA.3, 
“Common time frames for nationally determined contributions referred to 
in Article 4, paragraph 10, of the Paris Agreement,” adopted in 2021 at 
the 26th session of the Conference of the Parties. That decision “reaffirms” 
in paragraph 1 the nationally determined nature of nationally determined 
contributions and “encourages” in paragraph 2 the communication of 
targets for ten years after every five-year cycle. This language reflects only 
a soft form of obligation for each Party. Likewise, paragraph 170 of 
decision 1/CMA.5, relating to the global stocktake and adopted at the 28th 
session of the Conference of the Parties in 2023, maintained this softened 
form by “encouraging” Parties to communicate in 2025 their nationally 
determined contributions with an end date of 2035, pursuant to decision 
6/CMA.3.
- 103 -Depending on the type of greenhouse gas reduction measures, 
technological development and commercialization may require considerabl e 
time. Moreover, it also takes time for private actors, such as businesses, 
to respond to government policies in a way that leads to actual 
reductions in emissions. Therefore, specific reduction targets must be 
established well in advance of the target year. In addition, from the 
perspective of those engaged in each secto r—such as businesses that 
must develop business plans involving facility investments to reduce 
greenhouse gas emission s—the consistency of reduction policies is a 
critical factor that determines whether the actual emission targets can be 
achieved.
However, setting the target year of the nationally determined 
contributions, which are communicated every five years, as “ten years 
from the communication year” is not a legal obligation but, as noted 
above, falls within the discretionary domain of the government, subject 
to change depending on circumstances. Thus, it cannot be regarded as 
ensuring the preparation and implementation of a consistent policy 
pathway for greenhouse gas reductions from 2031.
b) Even if Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act, Article 4, paragraphs 9 and 10 of the Paris Agreement, and the 
above-mentioned decisions of the 26th session of the Conference of the 
Parties to the UNFCCC are followed and the “principle of progression” 
is applied, the “pace of progression” may still slow due to various 
real-world constraints. 
In our country, where the industrial structure is centered on manufacturing 
with many high greenhouse gas-emitting sectors, the government is likely 
to have numerous incentives to ease the burden of short-term reductions 
when setting greenhouse gas reduction targets in consideration of, inter 
alia, socioeconomic policies. If this results in a failure to accelerate the 
reduction rate, the pace of industrial restructuring will also slow down, 
potentially creating a vicious cycle in which the burden of future 
- 104 -reductions becomes increasingly heavier.
If the pace of upward progression in greenhouse gas reduction targets 
becomes excessively slow, the overall shape of the reduction pathway 
will take on a significantly convex form, resulting in a higher level of 
cumulative emissions and making the achievement of carbon neutrality 
by 2050 increasingly out of reach.
However, under the current structure of Article 8 of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, which provides no quantitative criteria for 
reduction targets from 2031—even in approximate terms—and leaves the 
setting of new targets every five years entirely to government discretion, 
it is difficult to identify any legal regulation capable of maintaining an 
appropriate “pace of progression.”
Article 8, Section (5) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act lists 
nine factors the government must consider when setting or modifying, 
inter alia, mid- to long-term reduction targets. Among them, the only 
factor that could realistically accelerate reduction efforts is Item 2, 
“National visions and national strategies,” which refers to the relatively 
long-term goal of “2050 carbon neutrality.” In contrast, the factor most 
likely to slow the pace of reduction is Item 6, “Impacts on the domestic 
industry, especially on the industry type and region highly dependent on 
fossil fuels,” which is relatively short-term and concrete. The remaining 
factors are neutral in nature and subject to change depending on 
circumstances. 
In particular, with respect to Item 9 of Article 8, Section (5) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act—“Trends in the international 
community’s response to the climate crisis ”—it may be insufficient as a 
substantive factor capable of accelerating national efforts to reduce 
greenhouse gas emissions, given the voluntary nature of nationally 
determined contributions under the Paris Agreement and the loose 
regulation surrounding the common time frame, which merely encourages 
the communication of targets for ten years from the communication year 
- 105 -on a five-year cycle. 
Even in 2016, when Article 25, Section (1) of the former Green 
Growth Act Enforcement Decree was amended to shift the target year 
for greenhouse gas reduction from 2020 to 2030—without strengthening 
the reduction target and amid a continued rise in actual emission s—the 
international discourse surrounding the UN climate regime still had an 
influence. Moreover, the 2023 Emissions Gap Report by the UNEP 
indicated that even if all countries fully implement their nationally 
determined contributions, the global average temperature is still projected 
to rise by 2.9 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, confirming, 
inter alia, that global reduction efforts remain insufficient. Given, inter 
alia, these circumstances, even under the framework of the UN climate 
regime, it is difficult to conclude that merely reexamining greenhouse 
gas reduction targets every five years, without legally establishing mid- 
to long-term reduction targets in advance, is sufficient to ensure that 
national targets are progressively strengthened at an appropriate pace.
c) Under Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act, the greenhouse gas reduction targets that the government is, in 
principle, required to re-examine every five years and modify or reset 
include not only mid- to long-term reduction targets but also sectoral and 
yearly reduction goals necessary to achieve them.
However, Article 8, Section (2) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act defines the sectoral reduction goals as a means to achieve the mid- 
to long-term reduction targets, while Section (3) defines the yearly 
reduction goals as a means to achieve both the mid- to long-term 
reduction targets and the sectoral reduction goals. As a result, all levels 
of targets are ultimately directed toward a single-year target designated 
every five years, without consideration of total emissions over the entire 
period leading up to that year. The numerical value of the mid- to 
long-term reduction target is determined based on the prevailing 
conditions and circumstances assessed at the time of each five-year 
- 106 -cycle. Moreover, no domestic procedure exists for calculating a carbon 
budget that could serve as a reference point for evaluating cumulative 
emissions.
Within this framework, if no quantitative criteri a—however approximate
—are established for specific reduction targets from 2031, the pace at 
which the targets are raised every five years is likely to depend solely on 
the short-term conditions and circumstances identified by the government 
at the time each target is set.
3) The specific and individual policy measures intended to achieve the 
mid- to long-term reduction target and the sectoral and yearly reduction 
goals under Article 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, are each 
implemented based on their own legal grounds set forth in other relevant 
statutes and regulations. The characteristics of each reduction measure 
also vary significantly. Integrating the targets and implementation 
management systems of individual sector s—each with distinct feature s—
into a single, unified legal framework is extremely difficult, and the 
current legal system is not designed to do so. In principle, the 
coordination and integration of these diverse reduction measures must be 
undertaken as part of the government’s authority and responsibility, 
through the formulation of a basic plan that includes the sectoral and 
yearly reduction goals along with corresponding implementation 
strategies, and through the continuous monitoring of their execution.
However, if the long-term, economy-wide national reduction targets 
leading up to the 2050 carbon neutrality goal rely entirely on the 
government’s holistic consideration of the characteristics of individual 
reduction measures and various surrounding circumstances at each point 
in time, the reduction targets for a given year and the overall reduction 
pathway for a given period may be heavily influenced depending on 
which sectoral characteristics the government chooses to focus its policy 
efforts on, making it difficult to ensure consistency in greenhouse gas 
reduction policy.
- 107 -In contrast, if reduction targets with at least some quantitative 
benchmarks are established for each interval leading up to the 2050 
carbon neutrality goal, the government would be able to determine 
specific targets by holistically considering the characteristics of individual 
reduction measures and various surrounding circumstances within that 
framework —through the coordination and integration of sectoral 
measures. This would make it possible to ensure a certain level of policy 
consistency while still respecting the government’s discretion in 
formulating greenhouse gas reduction policies.
Even when setting interim reduction targets for the period between 
2031 and 2049 in advance, the intervals between those targets and their 
specific numerical values fall within the legislature’s discretion. As with 
the reduction target set for 2030, a minimum reduction rate may be 
established by statute, while allowing for a certain degree of flexibility 
by delegating the determination of final figures to, inter alia, 
Presidential Decree.
Moreover, there are various possible approaches to establishing a 
target-setting system that considers cumulative emissions while ensuring 
progress in reduction amounts, and this too falls within the legislature’s 
discretion. For example, in addition to setting multiple single-year targets 
at various points leading up to carbon neutrality in 2050, a multi-year 
target approach may also be considered, whereby overall or average 
reduction rates are set for specified periods, thereby allowing for some 
degree of control over cumulative emissions.
4) Therefore, Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act sets only the reduction target rate up to 2030 and does not provide 
any form of quantitative criteria for the 19-year period from 2031 to 
2049. Considering the related legal framework, including the frequency 
and scope of greenhouse gas reduction target revisions under Section (4) 
of the same Article, there is no mechanism to effectively ensure gradual 
and continuous reductions up to the 2050 carbon neutrality target year. As 
- 108 -such, this constitutes regulation of reduction targets in a manner that 
shifts an excessive burden to the future, lacking the minimum 
characteristics required of a protective measure commensurate with the 
dangerous situation of the climate crisis.
(c) Regulation in case of failure to achieve reduction targets
1) Under the Paris Agreement framework, beginning in 2023, the 
Conference of the Parties shall conduct a global stocktake every five 
years to assess collective progress and communicate the results to the 
Parties (see Paris Agreement, Article 4, paragraphs 9, Article 14). Each 
Party submits, every two years, a “Biennial Transparency Report,” which 
includes both the National Inventory Repor t—containing country-specific 
statistics on anthropogenic emissions by sources and removals by sinks 
of greenhouse gases—and information necessary to track progress made 
in, inter alia, implementing its nationally determined contribution. These 
reports are subject to technical expert review (see, inter alia, Paris 
Agreement, Article 13, paragraphs 4, 7, 11).
Pursuant to Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act, the government, in principle, is required to reexamine and either 
modify or reset greenhouse gas reduction targets every five years, “in 
consideration of domestic and international conditions, such as the Paris 
Agreement,” and in accordance with the “principle of progression” under 
Article 4 of the Paris Agreement. For this reason, the outcomes of 
ongoing reviews conducted under the Paris Agreement framework may 
be reflected in the setting of the mid- to long-term reduction target and 
the sectoral and yearly reduction goals and in the formulation of 
corresponding implementation measures.
2)According to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, the implementation 
status of the “annual targets” and the progress of the “National Basic 
Plan,” which includes those targets, are qualitatively and quantitatively 
inspected and disclosed each year under the leadership of the 2050 
Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth Commission (hereinafter referred to 
- 109 -as the “Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth Commission”). The 
results of the inspection on the implementation of the National Basic 
Plan are reported annually to the National Assembly. If the amount of 
greenhouse gas emissions falls short of the yearly reduction goals, the 
head of the relevant administrative agency must prepare a reduction plan 
and submit it to the Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth Commission. 
Moreover, any shortcomings or areas for improvement identified in the 
implementation inspection report must be reflected in the policies etc. of 
the relevant administrative agencies or other entities (see Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, Articles 9, 13, 78). These procedures for 
inspecting the implementation status of yearly reduction goals and the 
progress of the National Basic Plan, inter alia, function as mechanisms 
to effectively ensure the implementation of the sectoral and yearly 
reduction goals.
3) The emissions trading system (hereinafter referred to as the “ETS”) 
under Article 25 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and the “Act 
on the Allocation and Trading of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Allowances” 
(hereinafter referred to as the “Emissions Trading Act”) is a system 
designed to effectively achieve the national greenhouse gas reduction 
targets by utilizing market mechanisms. Under this system, the 
government, taking into account the mid- to long-term reduction targets 
established pursuant to Article 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, 
sets a total allowable amount of greenhouse gas emissions for each 
five-year commitment period, within which emission permit s—representing 
the permitted quantity of emissions allocated to individual business entities 
producing greenhouse gases—may be traded (see Emissions Trading Act, 
Article 1, Article 2, Item 3, Article 5, Section (1), Item 1).
Under the Emissions Trading Act, the government is required to allocate 
emission permits to business entities producing greenhouse gases by 
dividing the timeline into five-year “commitment periods” and one-year 
“compliance years” within each commitment period, in order to achieve 
national greenhouse gas reduction targets and manage the outcomes of 
- 110 -compliance (Emissions Trading Act, Article 2, Sections (4), (5)). An 
entity holding emission permits may, with the approval of the competent 
authority, carry over such permits to the subsequent compliance year 
within the same commitment period or to the first compliance year of the 
next commitment period (Emissions Trading Act, Article 28, Section (1): 
Carryover of Emission Permits). If a business entity eligible for allocation 
is unable to fully fulfill its obligation to submit emission permits due to 
a shortfall between the number of permits held and the number required 
for submission for a completed compliance year, it may, within the limit 
prescribed by Presidential Decree and with the approval of the competent 
authority, borrow a portion of the emission permits allocated for another 
compliance year within the same commitment period (Emissions Trading 
Act, Articles 28, Sections (2), (3), Enforcement Decree of the same Act, 
Article 45: Borrowing of Emission Permits). In this way, greenhouse gas 
emissions in the sectors subject to the ETS are relatively strictly controlled 
across each five-year commitment period and each compliance year. 
Moreover, through the mechanisms of carryover and borrowing of 
emission permits, any failure to meet a reduction target within a given 
period is reflected in the subsequent period, thereby ensuring active control 
over the total volume of planned emissions.
Our country’s nationally determined contribution, submitted and 
registered with the UNFCCC Secretariat on December 23, 2021, states, 
with respect to the system or time frame for implementing the reduction 
target, that our country has set its greenhouse gas reduction target as a 
“single-year target” for 2030. It also states that, taking the 2030 target 
into account, our country has established the “3rd Basic Plan for the 
ETS” for 2021–2030, under which emission permits are allocated in 
five-year phases, accounting for 73.5% of total national emissions, to 
reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
In principle, the total volume of greenhouse gas emissions permitted 
under the national allocation plan, which is generally set for a five-year 
period, is determined in consideration of the mid- to long-term reduction 
- 111 -targets under Article 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act (see 
Emissions Trading Act, Article 2, Item 3, Article 5, Section (1), Item 1). 
In addition, the establishment and revision of the Basic Plan for the ETS 
and the national emissions allowance allocation plan must be finalized 
through deliberation by the Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth 
Commission and the State Council, except for minor matters prescribed 
by Presidential Decree (see Emissions Trading Act, Article 4, Section 
(5), Article 5, Section (5)). These provisions, inter alia, illustrate that 
the ETS is designed to be linked to the greenhouse gas reduction targets 
set forth in Article 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act. 
4) Complainants argue that the total volume of emissions for the entire 
target perio d—that is, the cumulative emission s—must be managed, and 
therefore the statute should provide for a rule that mandates the setting 
of additional targets in subsequent years when annual emissions exceed 
the existing targets.
If such additional plans are to be established, various policy 
considerations must be taken into account in determining whether the 
interval should be set as a one-year period in the following year or as a 
multi-year period, and whether the additional reduction targets should be 
regulated by sector or managed solely based on total annual emissions. 
This issue is also related to how the structure of target-setting should be 
governe d—whether by a “single-year target” model or a “multi-year 
target” model. In particular, if the failure to meet a target is not due to 
poor implementation by the responsible public authority, but instead 
stems from structural issues related to the private sector’s patterns of 
production and consumption, it may be necessary to consider that certain 
sectors face constraints that are difficult to overcome in the short term.
Under the framework of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, the 
goal of achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 remains unchanged (Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, Article 7, Section (1)). The government may 
modify or reset greenhouse gas reduction targets even before the lapse of 
- 112 -five years, if necessary due to changes in social and technical 
conditions etc., and such revisions are still subject to the “principle of 
progression” (see Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, Article 8, Section 
(4)). Given these provisions, how to reflect any shortfall in achieving the 
sectoral and yearly reduction goals for 2030 in future targets should be 
regarded as a matter of policy discretion for the government.
However, considering that the quantitative sectoral and yearly 
reduction goals, along with relatively detailed policy measures for their 
implementation, have already been established, and that the 2030 
reduction targets are set on the premise that they may be modified or 
reset in accordance with the principle of progression, it is difficult to 
conclude that the means of implementing the reduction targets are 
ineffective merely because the statute does not explicitly require 
shortfalls in annual targets to be reflected in subsequent plans, leaving 
such matters instead to the government’s policy discretion regarding the 
modification or resetting of greenhouse gas reduction targets.
From the perspective of managing the total permissible volume of 
greenhouse gas emissions (i.e., cumulative emissions) over a defined period 
of time, it is also difficult to deny that the ETS, which is based in principle 
on a “five-year commitment period” and incorporates mechanisms such as 
carryover and borrowing of emission permits, serves as a reasonably 
effective means of implementation. In that it manages total permissible 
emissions over each commitment period in a manner similar to a 
“multi-year target,” the ETS also serves to complement the “single-year 
target” approach adopted for mid- to long-term reduction targets.
The scope of application of the ETS in our country encompasses 73.5 
percent of the country’s total greenhouse gas emissions. However, given 
the nature of individual sectors, there may be areas to which the ETS is 
difficult to apply. Therefore, the fact that the ETS does not cover the entire 
scope of national emissions does not, by itself, mean that the system lacks 
institutional effectiveness.
- 113 -5) As examined above, the setting and implementation of greenhouse 
gas reduction targets under the framework of Article 8 of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act are subject to continuous review through the 
global stocktake process and the transparency mechanisms —such as the 
submission and review of biennial transparency report s—under the Paris 
Agreement. With respect to the yearly reduction goals, progress is 
reviewed each year primarily by the Carbon Neutrality and Green 
Growth Commission, and reduction plans addressing any unmet targets 
are prepared by the administrative agencies responsible for the respective 
sectors and incorporated into policy. In particular, in sectors subject to 
the ETS, the achievement of concrete emissions targets is effectively 
ensured through, inter alia, active management of the total allowable 
volume of emissions for each commitment period under the national 
emissions allocation plan, which is established in consideration of the 
mid- to long-term reduction targets set forth in Article 8 of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act.
When these means to ensure the achievement of emissions targets are 
considered together with the framework under Article 8, Section (4) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act governing the cycle, scope, and 
other aspects of resetting greenhouse gas reduction targets, it cannot be 
conclude d—solely on the ground that the statute does not explicitly 
provide that any shortfall in annual quantitative reduction targets must be 
added to subsequent target s—that the target-setting method under Article 
8, Section (1) is not effectively designed to ensure actual reductions in 
greenhouse gas emissions. Therefore, it cannot be said that the statutory 
framework fails to meet the minimum characteristics required of a 
protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of the 
climate crisis.
6) However, the abovementioned means to ensure the achievement of 
emissions targets cannot be considered effective mechanisms to guarantee 
gradual and continuous reductions during the period from 2031 to 2049, 
for which Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
- 114 -does not specify quantitative reduction targets. If the issue of reflecting 
unmet portions of quantitative reduction targets in subsequent targets is 
left solely to the government’s policy discretion at each point in time, 
there is a risk that progress toward reduction targets will be indefinitely 
postponed in the mid- to long term. 
In particular, the ETS’s ability to manage the total allowable volume 
of greenhouse gas emissions over a defined period is premised on the 
existence of quantitatively established mid- to long-term reduction targets 
and the sectoral and yearly reduction goals intended to achieve them. It 
is not designed to compensate for situations where greenhouse gas 
reduction targets have not been quantitatively set. This is illustrated by a 
past instance in which Article 25, Section (1) of the former Green 
Growth Act Enforcement Decree was amended in 2016, shifting the 
greenhouse gas reduction target year from 2020 to 2030. During that 
transition, additional emission allowances were added under the ETS. 
Specifically, according to Section 2, “Performance and Limitations,” of 
the government’s “3rd Basic Plan for the ETS” dated December 30, 
2019, during the first commitment period (2015–2017), a total emissions 
cap of 1,673 million tons was set by easing the reduction burden 
compared to the annual reduction rates under the “2020 Greenhouse Gas 
Reduction Roadmap,” in order to facilitate the initial establishment of the 
system. In 2017, the third compliance year of the first commitment 
period, the total emissions cap for that year was recalculated and 
increased by an additional 17.63 million tons in consideration of the 
2030 target and reduction measures set out in the “2030 Greenhouse Gas 
Reduction Roadmap (December 2016).”
(d) Sub-conclusion 
As the mid- to long-term reduction target set until 2030 under Article 
8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, 
Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree, the numerical reduction rate of 
reducing national greenhouse gas emissions by 40 percent compared to 
- 115 -2018 cannot be conclusively deemed to fall significantly short of our 
country’s fair share of global greenhouse gas reduction efforts or to 
impose an excessive burden on to the future in terms of the impacts of 
climate change and greenhouse gas emission restrictions.
With regard to means to ensure the achievement of emissions targets, 
such as the ETS and the review of the implementation status of the 
yearly reduction goals, it cannot be concluded that the method of setting 
greenhouse gas reduction targets under Article 8, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act is not effectively designed to 
guarantee greenhouse gas reductions solely because the statute does not 
explicitly require that any shortfall in annual quantitative reduction 
targets be incorporated into subsequent targets.
However, the fact that Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act does not present any form of quantitative criteria for 
reduction targets for the period from 2031 to 2049 means that it cannot 
effectively ensure gradual and continuous reductions leading up to the 
2050 carbon neutrality target year, and thus regulates greenhouse gas 
reduction targets in a manner that shifts an excessive burden to the 
future. 
Therefore, Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act violates the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection, as it 
lacks the minimum characteristics required of a protective measure 
commensurate with the dangerous situation of the climate crisis in its 
regulation of reduction targets for the period from 2031 to 2049.
3. Whether Article 8, Section (1) of Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act Violates Principle of Statutory Reservation 
(a) Reduction targets through 2030
The mid- to long-term reduction targets governed by Article 8, Section 
(1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act constitute measures to 
- 116 -protect Complainants in response to the climate crisis. Determining the 
specific figures and trajectory of the national greenhouse gas reduction 
targets falls within a specialized and technical domain based on scientific 
prediction and analysis, and there are limits to prescribing such matters 
in detail by statute. Furthermore, as mid- to long-term reduction targets 
are a core component of the nationally determined contributions that 
must be communicated under the Paris Agreement, the government’s 
diplomatic authority must also be respected in the process of specifying 
their content.
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
provides that, for the mid- to long-term reduction target through 2030, 
national greenhouse gas emissions shall be reduced by a percentag e—set 
by Presidential Decre e—of not less than 35 percent from 2018 levels, 
thereby establishing a quantitative minimum threshold for the 2030 target 
while delegating the determination of the specific figure to a Presidential 
Decree. In addition, Sections (2) and (3) of the same Article require the 
government to set sectoral and yearly reduction goals based on the mid- 
to long-term reduction target, taking into account various factors, 
including the characteristics of specific reduction measures, thereby 
specifying concrete reduction targets through 2030.
This approach reflects the fact that the setting of greenhouse gas 
reduction targets and trajectories falls not only within a scientific and 
technical domain but also requires consideration of a range of factors, 
including socioeconomic policies and diplomatic circumstances. Thus, the 
manner in which Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act regulates the reduction target through 2030 cannot be 
found to violate the principle of statutory reservation.
(b) Reduction targets from 2031 to 2049 
Establishing and planning greenhouse gas reduction targets and 
trajectories in response to the climate crisis constitutes policy formulation 
within a scientific and technical domain that must also take into account 
- 117 -various socioeconomic policies, diplomatic circumstances, and other 
factors. At the same time, it represents the implementation of the State’s 
duty to strive for environmental conservation under Article 35, Section 
(1) of the Constitution and entails broad and extensive restrictions on, 
inter alia, citizens’ freedom of occupation and exercise of property 
rights.
Based on the mid- to long-term reduction target established under 
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, the 
sectoral and yearly reduction goals and specific reduction measures are 
also determined. Given their significant impact on the national economy 
and industry, the government must comprehensively consider relevant 
factors when setting or modifying these targets. Furthermore, efforts 
must be made to hear and reflect the opinions of relevant experts, 
interested parties, etc. through public hearings and other means (see 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, Article 8, Sections (5), (6)). 
However, as previously noted, the failure of Article 8, Section (1) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act to set even an approximate 
quantitative level for the reduction targets for the period from 2031 to 
2049 results in a lack of the minimum characteristics required of a 
protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of the 
climate crisis, as it shifts an excessive burden to the future. The 
principal reason is that a structure in which specific reduction targets are 
determined solely based on the government’s situational awarenes s—
which may be short-ter m—cannot effectively ensure the proactiveness 
and consistency of greenhouse gas reduction policy.
Although the establishment of greenhouse gas reduction targets and the 
planning of corresponding reduction trajectories broadly and continuously 
restrict the fundamental rights of the people of the present generation, 
the nature of the climate crisis as a dangerous situation requires that the 
most ambitious reduction targets be set and continuously advanced so as 
not to shift an excessive burden to the future. A wide range of 
- 118 -conflicting interests may arise with respect to specific reduction 
measures. In light of these circumstances, the planning of mid- to 
long-term greenhouse gas reduction targets and trajectories is a domain 
that requires a very high level of social consensus. Therefore, even for 
the period from 2031, at least the general contours of such targets and 
trajectories must be directly prescribed by statut e—a norm that reflects 
the highest level of social consensus, apart from the Constitution. 
Legislator s—who are constituted through periodic election s—face a 
structural risk of failing to respond adequately to challenges such as the 
climate crisis, which require relatively long-term solutions. This concern 
is particularly acute given that so-called future generations, who are 
more severely affected by the impacts of the climate crisis, are largely 
excluded from the present democratic process. In this respect, the 
legislature bears an even greater obligation and responsibility to enact 
concrete legislation concerning mid- to long-term greenhouse gas 
reduction plans.
Therefore, the failure of Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act to prescribe even an approximate quantitative level for 
the reduction targets for the period from 2031 to 2049 constitutes a 
violation of the principle of statutory reservation, including the principle 
of parliamentary reservation.
4. Environmental Right Infringement and Holding of Decision
(a) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act
1) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
violates the State’s duty to protect fundamental rights by failing to set 
forth any quantitative level of reduction targets for the period from 2031 
to 2049. This violates the principle of prohibition of insufficient 
protection and the principle of statutory reservation, and thereby infringes 
Complainants’ environmental right.
- 119 -For the State’s setting of greenhouse gas reduction targets and 
implementation of various reduction measures to be meaningful as 
protective measures for fundamental rights, including Complainants’ 
environment right, in response to the irreversible and urgent crisis of 
climate change, they must actually result in reducing the cumulative 
amount of greenhouse gases emitted into the atmosphere to a level 
consistent with the target. In order for such protective measures to have 
even a minimum degree of effectiveness, the reduction targets must be 
quantitatively planned and their implementation properly managed; and 
in a rule-of-law State, such effectiveness must be secured through 
statute.
If the quantitative level of greenhouse gas reduction targets is not 
specified, it becomes impossible to assess whether reductions are being 
properly achieved, and the reduction measures cannot be managed, 
improved, or advanced.
Determining the specific numerical level of quantitative greenhouse gas 
reduction targets involves consideration of various scientific, policy, and 
diplomatic factors and diverse competing interests. Thus, it falls within 
the domain of the legislature and the executive body entrusted by it, in 
accordance with the principle of democracy. However, confirming and 
declaring the legislature’s duty to institutionalize such targets in an 
effective manner is the responsibility of the Court under the principle of 
the rule-of-law State. 
In particular, legislation setting greenhouse gas reduction targets has 
the character of limiting the fundamental rights of the present generation 
in order to safeguard the fundamental rights of future generations. Since 
the younger a generation is, the more constrained its participation in the 
democratic political process is, the intensity of judicial review with 
respect to the fulfillment of legislative obligations in this area must be 
heightened. The statement made by Complainant H.J. (age 12), who 
appeared at an oral argument of this case—“Adults can vote to elect 
- 120 -members of the National Assembly or the President, but children have 
no such opportunity. Participating in this case was the only action I 
could take—and had to take—for the future. ”—symbolically illustrates 
this point.
2) However, it is difficult to find that the specific reduction rate of the 
mid- to long-term reduction targets set forth in Article 8, Section (1) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act up to 2030 violates the principle 
of prohibition of insufficient protection or the principle of statutory 
reservation. If the entire normative scope of the provision were to be 
invalidated, even the existing quantitative interim targets prior to the 
2050 carbon neutrality goal would disappear. Consequently, the 
greenhouse gas reduction measures that are planned and being 
implemented based on those interim targets would lose their quantitative 
objectives, thereby causing a more unconstitutional situation in which the 
institutional framework for greenhouse gas reduction regresses.
Moreover, regarding the quantitative greenhouse gas reduction targets 
for the period from 2031 to 2049, the legislature has broad discretion in 
determining their levels and in prescribing the methods and frameworks 
for their statutory regulation. 
Therefore, instead of declaring Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act unconstitutional outright, it is necessary to 
issue a decision of nonconformity to the Constitution that orders the 
continued application of the provision until amended by February 28, 
2026. This decision takes into comprehensive consideration factors such 
as the schedule for submitting our country’s next nationally determined 
contribution, the approximately one year and two months period required 
for the National Assembly’s legislative process to repeal the former 
Green Growth Act and restructure the system by enacting the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, the institutional basis enabling the use of 
specialized and technical data, the urgency of the climate crisis 
necessitating efforts for rapid greenhouse gas reduction and timely policy 
- 121 -direction, and the time needed for the legislature to build social 
consensus on the overall targets of quantitative greenhouse gas reduction 
for the period from 2031 to 2049. However, if legislation is not 
amended by February 28, 2026, it should be noted that invalidating even 
the reduction targets set for 2030 would run counter to the purpose of 
the decision of nonconformity to the Constitution, which requires 
planning the quantitative levels of mid- to long-term greenhouse gas 
reduction targets and managing their implementation.
(b) Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act
Article 3, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
Enforcement Decree, enacted pursuant to the delegation under Article 8, 
Section (1) of the same Act, merely specifies the percentage for the mid- 
to long-term reduction target for 2030, and does not govern reduction 
targets or measures to ensure the achievement of emission targets for the 
years 2031 to 2049. Moreover, as discussed above, the specific 
numerical value of the 2030 mid- to long-term reduction target cannot, 
by itself, be deemed as lacking the minimum characteristics required of 
a protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of the 
climate crisis.
Therefore, Article 3, Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act does not violate the State’s duty to 
protect fundamental rights under the principle of prohibition of 
insufficient protection, and thus does not infringe Complainants’ 
environmental rights and other fundamental rights.
C. Analysis of Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue
1. Summary of Issues 
(a) The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue constitute an 
administrative plan outlining a specific pathway for greenhouse gas 
- 122 -reductions from 2023 to 2030. They serve both as a means to achieve 
the mid- to long-term reduction target for 2030 set forth in Article 8, 
Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, 
Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree, and as a domestic mitigation 
measure aimed at fulfilling our country’s nationally determined 
contribution under the Paris Agreement. 
Where an administrative plan concerning the pathway for greenhouse 
gas reductions is formulated in a manner that shifts an excessive burden 
to the future, it lacks the minimum characteristics required of a 
protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of the 
climate crisis.
Furthermore, if an administrative plan established to achieve the 
greenhouse gas reduction target set by law is structured in such a way 
that the target cannot in fact be attained, it becomes evident that the 
legal targets cannot lead to an actual reduction of that amount. Such an 
administrative plan, in terms of its effectiveness as a legal mechanism 
for regulating mitigation measures against the climate crisis, lacks the 
minimum characteristics required of a protective measure commensurate 
with the dangerous situation of the climate crisis.
Whether the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue violate 
the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection must be determined 
by considering the above issues in light of scientific facts and 
international standards.
(b) With respect to the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue, Complainants argue that a concave reduction pathway has been 
established, which increases cumulative greenhouse gas emissions and 
places a heavier burden on the future due to the shares of the industrial 
sector, carbon capture, utilization, and storage (hereinafter referred to as 
“CCUS”) sector, and the international reduction sector. They further 
contend that excessive reliance on uncertain reduction measures has 
significantly lowered the likelihood of achieving the 2030 reduction 
- 123 -target.
Thus, the issue is whether the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets 
at Issue, as an administrative plan concerning the greenhouse gas 
reduction pathway and reduction measures, have been established in a 
way that shifts an excessive burden to the future, and whether they are 
designed so as to make it impossible to achieve the 2030 mid- to 
long-term reduction target set forth in Article 8, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree.
(c) The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are based on 
the 2018 national greenhouse gas emissions figure of 727.6 million tons 
as the “gross emissions,” while the 2030 national greenhouse gas 
emission targe t—which requires a 40% reduction from that baselin e—is 
set at 436.6 million tons based on “net emissions,” calculated by 
subtracting absorption and removal from gross emissions.
The issue is whether this method of calculating the emission target is 
permissible in light of, inter alia, the framework of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, and whether the Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue constitute an administrative plan designed to 
achieve the 2030 mid- to long-term reduction target set forth in Article 
8, Section (1) of this Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement 
Decree.
The issue regarding the method of calculating the emission targets is 
not only whether the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue 
violate the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection, but also 
whether, as an administrative plan established pursuant to Article 8, 
Sections (2) and (3) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act to achieve 
the mid- to long-term reduction target under Section (1) of the same 
article, it violates the principle of statutory supremacy in rule-of-law 
based administration. 
- 124 -(d) Complainants argue that the Basic Plan at Issue, which sets a 
20-year planning period under Article 10, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, violates that provision because the Sectoral 
and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue were finalized in 2023 but only 
set plans through 2030, without establishing plans for the period from 
2031 to 2042. However, the 20-year planning period prescribed in 
Article 10, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
encompasses the entire plan, which includes not only greenhouse gas 
reductions classified as “mitigation measures” in response to the climate 
crisis, but also other measures such as “adaptation measures” specified in 
each Item of Section (2) of the same Article. The Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue correspond to the sectoral reduction goals 
under Article 8, Section (2), and the yearly reduction goals under 
Section (3) of the same Act. These targets serve as prerequisites for the 
“sectoral and annual measures to attain the mid- to long-term reduction 
targets, etc.” under Article 10, Section (2), Item 4. They are premised on 
the mid- to long-term reduction target under Article 8, Section (1) of the 
same Act, which aims at 2030. Therefore, because emission targets for 
the period from 2031 are not subject to be set by the Sectoral and 
Annual Reduction Targets at Issue under the relevant statutes and 
regulations, the argument that the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets 
at Issue violate Article 10, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act will not be further examined.
Complainants argue that the Plans at Issue lack annual measures for 
reducing greenhouse gas emissions. However, specific sectoral and 
annual measures to achieve the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets 
at Issue are set forth in detail in “1. Sectoral Mid- to Long-Term 
Reduction Measures” under “VI. Tasks for Implementing the National 
Basic Plan,” based on what is established in “D. Directions for Major 
Sectoral Reductions” under “V. Mid- to Long-Term Reduction Targets” 
of the Basic Plan at Issue. The timing of implementation is also 
indicated for each individual and specific reduction measure. Therefore, 
- 125 -it cannot be said that the Plans at Issue lack annual reduction 
measures; rather, it simply does not adopt a format that subdivides 
reduction measures by each year. 
Complainants argue that the Plans at Issue lack a reduction plan in the 
event of failure to meet the annual reduction targets, which essentially 
amounts to a claim that there is no provision for adding unmet portions 
to subsequent reduction targets if specific emission targets are not 
achieved. The Basic Plan at Issue was formulated on the premise of 
applying the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and other relevant 
statutes and regulations. Having already addressed this argument with 
respect to Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act, no separate judgment will be made regarding the Sectoral and 
Annual Reduction Targets at Issue. 
2. Whether Prohibition of Insufficient Protection Has Been Violated 
with Respect to Reduction Pathway and Reduction Measures 
(a) Complainants argue that the cumulative reduction amounts under 
the Annual Reduction Targets at Issue for the period 2028 to 2030 are 
approximately three times greater than those for 2023 to 2027. Such a 
convex reduction pathway, which concentrates the burden of greenhouse 
gas reductions in the latter period, increases cumulative emissions 
compared to a linear reduction pathway and imposes an excessive burden 
by shifting the reduction responsibility to subsequent governments and 
future generations. 
In particular, Complainants argue that considering factors such as our 
country’s renewable energy potential and the necessity of proactive 
demand management of energy consumption, it is not impossible to plan 
and implement a concave reduction pathway that achieves rapid 
greenhouse gas reductions at relatively low initial costs. They further 
contend that in balancing interests, greater weight must be given to the 
potentially severe damages resulting from inadequate greenhouse gas 
- 126 -reductions rather than to the short-term burdens currently imposed.
Since the level of global average temperature increase is proportional 
to the cumulative amount of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, it is 
desirable that the greenhouse gas reduction pathway minimizes 
cumulative emissions as much as possible. Moreover, to avoid shifting 
an excessive reduction burden to the future, the annual reduction rates 
should not be excessively high. Therefore, as Complainants argue, a 
greenhouse gas reduction pathway that approximates a concave shape is 
ideally preferable.
However, depending on the type of greenhouse gas reduction 
measures, considerable time may be required for technology development 
and commercialization, and there may be a time lag between the full 
implementation of policies and the realization of their effects. Therefore, 
from the perspective of practical feasibility, there are sectors for which a 
convex-shaped reduction pathway is unavoidable, and there are also 
cases where the reduction pathways of individual sectors have 
interrelations with those of other sectors.
In addition, transforming both the energy supply system and the 
overall structure of the industrial sector requires large-scale capital 
investment in facilities. When considering demand-side management as 
well, concerns may arise over a sharp increase in energy costs. From the 
perspective of policymakers, this may call for a consideration of various 
socioeconomic factors, such as price stability.
Ultimately, the form of the reduction pathway, which consists of 
specific annual emission reduction targets, is determined by considering 
together the concrete characteristics of available reduction measures by 
sector, various technical and economic conditions, and the interrelations 
between individual reduction measures and sectors. Pursuant to Article 4, 
paragraph 1 of the Paris Agreement, each country is required to plan a 
pathway for rapid reductions in accordance with best available science. 
However, the specific form of such a reduction pathway must, in 
- 127 -principle, be determined under the authority and responsibility of the 
government, through the selection and adjustment of sector-specific 
reduction measures.
Therefore, whether the planned reduction pathway, as set out in the 
Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, constitutes a violation of 
the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection on the ground that 
it shifts an excessive burden to the future must be assessed based on 
whether there was any illegality or manifest abuse of discretion in the 
government’s exercise of authority in selecting and coordinating 
sector-specific reduction measures, as the factor determining the form of 
the reduction pathway.
(b) Complainants argue that the reduction in the share of emission cuts 
allocated to the industrial sector in the Sectoral Reduction Targets at 
Issue, compared to the distribution announced by the government in 
2021 when presenting the mid- to long-term reduction target for 2030, 
violates the polluter-pays principle and, in light of the global trend 
toward decarbonization, rather undermines industrial competitiveness.
However, the sector-specific reduction distribution announced by the 
government in 2021 was not specified in numerical terms in the 
nationally determined contribution submitted by our country to the 
UNFCCC Secretariat. Moreover, it did not constitute a formal plan 
grounded in statutes or regulations, but rather reflected a proposal 
considered in the course of policy deliberation when preparing the 
nationally determined contribution enhancement plan. As such, the 
Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue, which were subsequently finalized 
based on applicable statutes and regulations, may be understood as 
involving partial adjustments to the reduction shares among sectors.
The “polluter-pays principle” referred to in Article 3, Item 5 of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act is concerned with “reforming, inter 
alia, the taxation and financial systems so that the economic costs of 
environmental pollution and greenhouse gas emissions are reasonably 
- 128 -reflected in the market prices of goods or services.” It is difficult to 
view this principle as being directly connected to the reduction share. 
Moreover, no clear standard exists for determining the appropriate level 
of emission reductions for each sector under the polluter-pays principl e—
one that would be free from reasonable objection.
The policy appropriateness of matters raised by Complainant s—such as 
the actual reduction burden on the industrial sector, its reduction 
potential, and the maintenance of industrial competitivenes s—is 
insufficient to serve as grounds for finding that the government’s 
exercise of authority in coordinating the sectoral targets under the 
Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue involved any illegality or 
manifest abuse of discretion.
(c) The main reason the Annual Reduction Targets at Issue follow a 
convex trajectory, with progressively larger annual reduction targets 
toward the end of the period, is that absorption through CCUS is 
reflected only from 2026 onward, and international reductions are applied 
solely to the 2030 target.
In this regard, Complainants argue that such a pathway excessively 
relies on uncertain means of reduction, thereby significantly lowering the 
likelihood of achieving the 2030 target. They further contend that, since 
international reductions require substantial public funding, allocating a 
smaller portion of the reductions to the industrial sector while increasing 
reliance on international reductions runs counter to the polluter-pays 
principle.
Absorption through CCUS is not yet technologically or economically 
mature compared to reduction measures in other sectors. Moreover, in 
the cases of carbon capture and utilization and international reductions, 
there remain areas where international consensus has yet to be reached 
regarding the methodology for calculating reduction amounts. As such, 
methods of absorption and removal that rely on CCUS and international 
reductions present greater uncertainty at this stage in terms of making 
- 129 -concrete predictions about the amount of reductions they can achieve.
However, CCUS technologies are included as greenhouse gas reduction 
measures under Article 34 (Fostering of Technology for Carbon Capture, 
Use, and Storage) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, while 
international reductions through absorption and removal methods are 
included under Article 35 (Implementation of International Mitigation 
Projects) of the same Act. CCUS technologies are also identified as one 
of the key reduction measures to achieve the 1.5°C target in Paragraph 
28(e) of Decision 1/CMA.5 on the Global Stocktake adopted at the 28th 
Conference of the Parties to the UNFCCC. International reductions are 
grounded in Article 6 of the Paris Agreement.
The main clause of Article 8, Section (4) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act states that “the government shall re-examine mid- to 
long-term reduction targets, sectoral reduction targets, and annual 
reduction targets every five years and shall modify or reset such targets 
in accordance with the principle of progression under Article 4 of the 
Agreement, if necessary.” Its proviso adds: “Provided , That if it is 
necessary due to changes in social and technical conditions etc., the 
targets may be modified or reset before five years elapse.” The Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue note that “international reductions will be 
reflected only in the 2030 target; annual targets for international 
reductions will be set taking into account the finalization of relevant 
international standards, the expected initial year of utilization (2026), and 
other relevant factors.” Given these circumstances, once an international 
consensus on, inter alia, how to calculate reduction amounts is reached, 
the annual targets for the international-reduction sector may be 
reestablished after the first year of utilization. In that event, the convex 
shape of the reduction pathway created by the Annual Reduction Targets 
at Issue could be moderated.
CCUS technologies are separately itemized under the absorption and 
removal category in the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, 
- 130 -but they also have the potential to serve as one of several reduction 
measures within the energy transition sector or the industrial sector. The 
international reduction sector is an area linked to multiple emission 
sectors, and the Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue state that 
“international reductions are to be utilized as a supplementary means to 
domestic reductions and pursued in a manner that contributes to global 
carbon mitigation efforts, such as under the Paris Agreement.” As 
previously noted, no standard has been identified that would render the 
allocation of the reduction burden across these respective domains 
difficult to contest under the polluter pays principle.
In light of the foregoing, it is difficult to conclude that the government 
acted unlawfully or clearly exceeded its discretionary authority merely 
based on the proportions assigned to each sector, and on the fact that it 
planned a specific reduction pathway by adjusting the shares of various 
mitigation measures —including CCUS and international reduction s—
which, although permitted under domestic law, international treaties, and 
international consensus, still involve uncertainties with respect to, inter 
alia, the quantification of specific reduction amounts. 
(d) Therefore, it is difficult to find that the Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue involve any illegality or manifest abuse of 
discretion in the government’s exercise of authority concerning the 
selection and coordination of reduction measures across sectors. Thus, 
the mere shape of the resulting reduction pathway does not, by itself, 
justify the conclusion that an excessive burden has been shifted to the 
future.
The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue maintain the same 
total post-reduction emissions target as that set in 2021 when the 
enhanced nationally determined contribution was formulated, while 
adjusting the shares of the industrial sector and the CCUS sector and the 
international reduction sectors. Absent any finding of illegality or 
manifest abuse of discretion in this regard, the adjustment of these 
- 131 -sectoral weights alone cannot support the conclusion that the targets 
were designed in a manner that renders it impossible to achieve the mid- 
to long-term reduction target set under Article 8, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree.
Given that the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, from 
the perspective of planning the reduction pathway and reduction 
measures, cannot be said to lack the minimum characteristics required of 
a protective measure commensurate with the dangerous situation of the 
climate crisis, the Court cannot find that they violate the principle of 
prohibition of insufficient protection.
3. Whether Method of Calculating Emissions Target Has Violated 
Principle of Prohibition of Insufficient Protection or Principle of 
Statutory Supremacy
(a) Opinion of rejection by Justices Lee Jongseok, Lee Eunae, Lee 
Youngjin, and Kim Hyungdu
1) The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are intended to 
achieve the mid- to long-term reduction target, and Article 8, Section (1) 
of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, which provides the basis for 
the mid- to long-term target, uses the term “national greenhouse gas 
emissions.” The question is whether the term “emissions” in Article 8, 
Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act refers to either 
gross emissions or net emissions, or to net emissions alone, and whether, 
on that basis, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are 
inconsistent with higher-level laws.
a) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act uses 
only the general term “emissions,” without specifying whether it refers to 
“gross emissions” or “net emissions.” Article 3, Section (1) of the 
Enforcement Decree of the same Act, which was adopted pursuant to the 
delegation of authority under Article 8, Section (1), also prescribes only
- 132 - the reduction rate of 40 percent without referring to emissions at all. 
Moreover, neither the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act nor its 
Enforcement Decree defines the meaning of “emissions” or sets out a 
method for calculating it. However, Article 2, Item 3 of the Act defines 
“carbon neutrality” as a “state in which net emissions, obtained by 
offsetting the amount of greenhouse gases absorbed from the amount of 
greenhouse gases emitted, discharged, or leaked into the atmosphere, are 
zero.” In this definition, the term “net emissions” is used explicitly. 
Nevertheless, Article 8, Section (1), which concerns the national 
greenhouse gas reduction target to achieve such carbon neutrality, uses 
only the general term “emissions.” This suggests that the term 
“emissions” in Article 8, Section (1) cannot be definitively interpreted to 
mean “net emissions” alone.
b) The Carbon Neutrality Framework Act was enacted by adopting an 
alternative bill proposed by the Environment and Labor Committee, 
which integrated and harmonized eight legislative bills concerning 
climate change response proposed by members of the National 
Assembly. Among these bills, six contained provisions regarding “mid- 
to long-term reduction targets” corresponding to Article 8, Section (1) of 
the current Carbon Neutrality Framework Act. Of those six, four used 
the term “total greenhouse gas emissions,” one used “greenhouse gas 
emissions,” and one simply stated that “a mid- to long-term national 
greenhouse gas reduction target shall be established,” without further 
elaboration. It appears that, in the process of adopting the committee’s 
alternative bill, there was no discussion concerning the specific meaning 
of “emissions.”
c) Article 25, Section (1) of the 2019 Enforcement Decree of the 
former Framework Act on Low Carbon, Green Growth —now repealed 
upon the enforcement of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act—
prescribed that “the greenhouse gas reduction target under Article 42, 
Section (1), Item 1 of the Act shall be to reduce the total national 
greenhouse gas emissions in 2030 by 24.4 percent of the total national 
- 133 -greenhouse gas emissions in 2017,” thereby using the term “total 
emissions” for both the base year 2017 and the target year 2030. 
However, Section (2) of the same Article provided that, “when 
calculating the performance of achieving the reduction target under 
Section (1), reductions from overseas reduction efforts through 
international carbon markets, etc. and from carbon sinks under Article 
55, Sections (2) and (3) shall be included.” Since reductions through 
carbon sinks and other measures are to be included when calculating the 
total emissions at the reduction target point specified in Section (1), 
there is room to interpret the “total emissions” for the target year 2030 
as effectively referring to “net emissions.” This illustrates that, even 
where the same term is used within a provision, it does not necessarily 
carry the same meaning in all instances and may be interpreted 
differently depending on context. Although neither the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act nor its Enforcement Decree contains a provision like 
Article 25, Section (2) of the former Enforcement Decree, they likewise 
do not define “emissions” or specify how emissions are to be calculated 
in detail. Therefore, the possibility remains that the same term may be 
interpreted differently depending on context.
d) According to the nationally determined contribution submitted by 
our country under the Paris Agreement, the base year is designated as 
2018, with the corresponding emissions stated as “727.6 million tons,” 
with “excluding LULUCF” (Land Use, Land Use Change and Forestry; 
see Article 2, Item 9 of the Act on the Preservation and Promotion of 
Carbon Sinks; hereinafter referred to as “LULUCF”) indicated alongside 
the figure. The nationally determined contribution presents a target of 
reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 40 percent from the “total national 
greenhouse gas emissions” in 2018 by the year 2030, stating that efforts 
will include the LULUCF sector, though without specifying the concrete 
methods for doing so. Therefore, the indication of “emissions (total)” in 
the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue reflects an approach 
that, in the absence of a definition of emissions in the Carbon Neutrality 
- 134 -Framework Act and its Enforcement Decree, generally aligns with the 
method presented in our country’s nationally determined contributio n—
namely, using gross emissions for the base year and net emissions for 
the target year. Thus, this method of calculation cannot be deemed a 
violation of Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework 
Act.
e) As seen above, our country has consistently pursued the 2030 
national greenhouse gas reduction target and its implementation under, 
inter alia, Article 42, Section (1) of the former Framework Act on Low 
Carbon, Green Growth and Article 25, Section (1) of its 2019 
Enforcement Decree, both of which were repealed upon the enactment of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act. Therefore, it is necessary to 
maintain the standard of using total emissions in the base year as 
adopted under the previous law.
f) As provided in Article 7, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act, the government aims to achieve carbon neutrality by 
2050. Since carbon neutrality refers to a state in which net greenhouse 
gas emissions are zero, it is reasonable to interpret the “emissions” for 
the interim target year of 2030 as net emissions. Furthermore, Article 2, 
Item 7 of the same Act defines “greenhouse gas reduction” as “all kinds 
of activities to reduce or absorb greenhouse gas emissions to alleviate or 
delay climate change.” Therefore, greenhouse gas absorption activities, in 
addition to reduction activities, should be reflected in setting the “target” 
year for reduction. However, 2018 is a point in the past and holds 
significance only as a reference for assessing whether a specific target 
has been achieved. Thus, even though the statutory language simply 
refers to “emissions,” there is no compelling reason to interpret it 
exclusively as “net emissions.”
g) Furthermore, it may be pointed out that, according to the data for 
the carbon sink sector in the Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, the 
absorption in the base year of 2018 is recorded as “–41.3 million tons,” 
- 135 -whereas for the target year of 2030 it is “–26.7 million tons,” 
indicating a decrease in absorption. The annual absorption targets from 
2023 to 2030 also show a consistent downward trend. If absorption is 
not reflected in the base year but only in the target year, this could 
create a distortion, making it appear as though national greenhouse gas 
emissions have been reduced due to an increase in absorption, when in 
fact absorption has declined. However, this criticism ultimately arises 
from the fact that the “emissions (total)” for the base year of 2018—the 
reference point for compariso n—represent only gross emissions, 
excluding the “absorption and removal” component. As discussed above, 
given that the statutory language refers simply to “emissions,” there is 
no compelling reason to interpret it strictly as “net emissions.” 
Accordingly, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue cannot 
be deemed “unlawful” on that basis.
h) Considering these factors together, the term “emissions” in Article 
8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act cannot be 
interpreted as referring to either gross emissions or net emissions, nor 
can it be interpreted as referring solely to net emissions. Therefore, 
based on such an interpretation, it cannot be concluded that the Sectoral 
and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue have violated the higher laws.
2) The Court has examined whether the State has fulfilled its duty to 
protect the environmental right by determining “whether it has taken at 
least appropriate and effective minimum protective measures ”—that is, 
based on the principle of the prohibition of insufficient protection. 
Whether this principle has been violated must be assessed in a 
case-specific manner, by weighing such factors as the type of legal 
interest at issue, the status of that interest within the constitutional order, 
the nature and degree of its infringement or the risk posed thereto, and 
the significance of any competing legal interests (see Constitutional 
Court 2018Hun-Ma730, December 27, 2019).
Climate change constitutes a concrete situation of danger that threatens 
- 136 -the lives, personal safety, and other interests of citizens, including 
Complainants, and undermines the environment that forms the basis of 
daily life. There is no dispute that responding to this requires an 
approach grounded in professional and scientific forecasting and analysis, 
and that international cooperation is essential to resolving the climate 
crisis. Against this backdrop, it must be examined whether recording the 
“emissions (total)” for 2018 as gross emissions and the “emissions 
(total)” for 2030 as net emissions, and setting the sectoral and yearly 
reduction goals based on a 40 percent figure calculated on that basis, 
infringes Complainants’ environmental right and other fundamental rights 
in violation of the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection, 
which the Court has adopted as its standard of review in prior decisions.
a) Given that Article 7, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act sets the goal of achieving “carbon neutrality” by 2050, 
the figure of 40 percent set forth in Article 8, Section (1) of the same 
Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree can be 
regarded as a transitional step toward carbon neutrality. If both the 
“emissions (total)” for 2018 and 2030 are interpreted as “net emissions,” 
the resulting reduction target may fall somewhat short of 40 percent. 
However, this slight numerical shortfall alone does not mean that the 
framework was designed to preclude the achievement of carbon 
neutrality, nor can it be concluded that the legal system governing 
mitigation measures against the climate crisis lacks even the “minimum” 
level of effectiveness. The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue provide for the gradual annual reduction of emissions by sector,
