while also taking into consideration the potential decline in absorption 
capacity resulting from forest aging. Although the future cannot be 
predicted with certainty, it is reasonable to assume that science and 
technology for mitigating the climate crisis will advance, and as long as 
international consensus on the climate crisis continues to expand, it 
should be deemed permissible for administrative plans to reflec t—in 
quantitative terms—the potential increase in “absorption and removal” 
- 137 -premised on technological development and expanded international 
cooperation for addressing the climate crisis. Therefore, it is difficult to 
conclude that the current projections are excessively set. On this basis, it 
is evident that the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are 
aimed at addressing the climate crisis and that they establish specific 
mitigation goals that are, at least on their face, reasonable. Thus, it 
cannot be said that the State has failed to fulfill even the minimum level 
of its obligation to guarantee the right to live in a healthy and pleasant 
environment.
b) Although the 2018 “emissions (total)” —which serve as the reference 
point for the Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue—are recorded as “gross 
emissions” without incorporating “absorption and removal” figures, 
thereby failing to reflect the declining trend in greenhouse gas absorption 
due to factors such as forest aging, it cannot be concluded on that basis 
alone that the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are 
inconsistent with the “transparency” sought by the Paris Agreement. As 
previously noted, our country indicated its 2018 greenhouse gas 
emissions as “727.6 million tons” in its nationally determined contribution, 
explicitly stating that the figure is “excluding LULUCF.” 
c) According to the precedents of the Court, where an alleged 
infringement of Complainants’ environmental rights is reviewed from the 
perspective of the State’s duty to protect fundamental rights, the relevant 
standard is whether the State has taken at least appropriate and effective 
minimum protective measures. Given that Article 7, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act sets the goal of achieving “carbon 
neutrality” by 2050, the 40 percent reduction set forth in Article 8, 
Section (1) of the same Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its 
Enforcement Decree constitutes an intermediate step toward carbon 
neutrality. Considering the administrative plans to be established in the 
future, the possibility of achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 remains 
open. Therefore, even if the “emissions (total),” as used in the Sectoral 
and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, are interpreted as “net 
- 138 -emissions,” and a reduction target slightly short of 40 percent would 
be set as a result, it is difficult to conclude on that basis alone that the 
State “has failed to implement even the minimum protective measures” 
required to safeguard environmental rights. In fact, our country has been 
making efforts toward carbon neutrality even prior to the enactment of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, and the Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue clearly aim to achieve carbon neutrality and 
ultimately to address the climate crisis by continuously enhancing and 
expanding “greenhouse gas absorption” activities as part of such ongoing 
efforts. Therefore, it cannot be said that the State has failed to fulfill 
even the minimum of its obligation to guarantee the right to live in a 
healthy and pleasant environment.
3) As discussed above, there is no clear basis for prohibiting the 
interpretation of the “emissions (total)” for the reference year 2018 as 
gross emissions and the “emissions (total)” for the target year 2030 as 
net emissions under Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act. Thus, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue, which were established by setting the sectoral and yearly reduction 
goals at a 40 percent reduction based on that interpretation, do not 
violate Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act or 
Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement Decree. Moreover, insofar as 
the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are aimed at 
addressing the climate crisis and appear to reasonably establish specific 
mitigation goals, they cannot be deemed to violate the principle of 
prohibition of insufficient protection.
(b) Opinion of unconstitutionality by Justices Kim Kiyoung, Moon 
Hyungbae, Lee Mison, Jung Jungmi, and Cheong Hyungsik
1) Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
provides that “The government shall set a national mid- to long-term 
greenhouse gas emission reduction target (hereinafter referred to as 
‘mid-to long-term reduction target’) to reduce national greenhouse gas 
- 139 -emissions by a ratio prescribed by Presidential Decree within the range 
of not less than 35 percent from the 2018 level by 2030.” As such, 
Article 8, Section (1) uses the same term, “national greenhouse gas 
emissions,” twice—once for the year 2018 and once for the year 2030—
without providing any additional explanation. Based on the plain text of 
the statutory provision, the “emissions” for those two years cannot be 
interpreted using different standards.
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act sets the 
quantitative level of the greenhouse gas reduction target as a percentage 
reduction in national greenhouse gas emissions by the target year relative 
to a base year. From this provision, a calculation formula can be derived 
by which the government determines the specific emissions target. In 
terms of this formul a—where the reduction target is defined as a 
percentag e—the input value and the output value cannot be based on 
different standards.
The quantitative level of greenhouse gas reduction targets must be 
prescribed by statute, at least in broad terms. The main reason for 
concluding that the failure to directly prescribe by statute the quantitative 
level of the greenhouse gas reduction targets for the period from 2031 to 
2049 in Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
violates the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection and the 
principle of statutory reservation is that, if the setting of such 
quantitative targets were left entirely to the discretion of the government, 
there would be a risk that the government might focus only on the 
short-term reduction burdens, thereby increasing long-term burden and 
undermining the consistency of climate policy. Consequently, such 
measures would fail to secure the institutional effectiveness required of a 
protective measure commensurate with the climate crisis. If, under the 
government’s interpretation, it were possible to alter the framework for 
quantitative reduction targets already established by Article 8, Section (1) 
of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act in order to set specific 
emissions targets, the statutory provision itself, which establishes such a 
- 140 -framework, would lose the institutional effectiveness constitutionally 
required of it.
When the emissions targets in the Sectoral and Annual Reduction 
Targets at Issue are converted into reduction rates relative to the base 
year, the outcome varies depending on the standard used for calculation: 
under the “gross emissions –net emissions” basis, the reduction amounts 
to 40 percent; under the “net emissions –net emissions” basis, it amounts 
to 36.4 percent; and under the “gross emissions –gross emissions” basis, 
it results in a 29.6 percent reduction. By applying different standards for 
the base year and the target year and calculating the emissions target 
using the “gross emissions –net emissions” standard, the government, in 
setting the single-year reduction target for 2030, arbitrarily altered the 
framework established by Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act, which refers to “national greenhouse gas emissions” in 
identical terms for both the base year 2018 and the target year 2030 and 
quantifies the “reduction rate” between them. As a result, the actual level 
of reduction was effectively lowered, thereby impairing the institutional 
effectiveness of the mid- to long-term reduction target as set forth in 
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act.
Therefore, in establishing administrative plans to achieve the mid- to 
long-term reduction target set forth in Article 8, Section (1) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, it is impermissible to calculate 
sectoral and annual targets for emissions and absorption using the 
standard of “gross emissions for the base year–net emissions for the 
target year,” as done in the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue.
2) Article 2, Item 3 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act defines 
“carbon neutrality” as “a state in which net emissions, obtained by 
offsetting the amount of greenhouse gases absorbed from the amount of 
greenhouse gases emitted, discharged, or leaked into the atmosphere, are 
zero,” thereby employing the concept of “net emissions.”
- 141 -Further, the mid- to long-term reduction target set forth in Article 8, 
Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act serves as an 
intermediate target toward achieving the national vision of “carbon 
neutrality by 2050,” as prescribed in Article 7, Section (1) of the same 
Act. The goal of “carbon neutrality by 2050” also serves as an important 
premise in assessing the following: the constitutionality of the reduction 
percentage prescribed in Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act—namely, “a ratio prescribed by Presidential Decree 
within the range of not less than 35 percent from the 2018 level”; the 
constitutionality of the “40 percent” figure prescribed in Article 3, 
Section (1) of the Enforcement Decree of the same Act; and the 
constitutionality of the government’s failure to set quantitative reduction 
targets for the period from 2031 to 2049.
Moreover, Article 3, Section (4) of the Enforcement Decree of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act provides that “where the government 
sets, modifies, or resets the mid- to long-term greenhouse gas reduction 
target, etc. pursuant to Article 8 (1) through (4) of the Act, it may take 
into account the greenhouse gas reduction performance achieved by 
utilizing carbon sinks etc. under Article 33 (1) of the Act and the 
international reduction outcomes etc. under the main clause of Article 35 
(3) of the Act.”
Additionally, Article 25, Sections (1) and (2) of the former Green 
Growth Act Enforcement Decree were not based on the concept of 
“carbon neutrality.” Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act refers only to the “national greenhouse gas emissions,” 
whereas Article 25, Section (1) of the former Green Growth Act 
Enforcement Decree referred to the “total national greenhouse gas 
emissions.” In addition, Article 3, Section (4) of the Enforcement Decree 
of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act provides that, where the 
government sets, modifies, or resets the mid- to long-term greenhouse 
gas reduction target etc., it “may take into account” the “reduction 
performance achieved by utilizing carbon sinks etc.” whereas Article 25, 
- 142 -Section (2) of the former Green Growth Act Enforcement Decree 
stated that, “when calculating performance in achieving the reduction 
target,” the “amount reduced through the use of carbon sinks shall be 
included.” These provisions are based on different regulatory structures. 
As such, the content of the former Green Growth Act Enforcement 
Decree, as described above, does not influence the interpretation of 
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act.
In light of these considerations, when the government sets sectoral and 
yearly reduction goals pursuant to Article 8, Sections (2) and (3) of the 
Carbon Neutrality Framework Act in order to achieve the reduction rate 
set forth in Section (1) of the same Article and Article 3, Section (1) of 
the same Act’s Enforcement Decree, it would be reasonable to calculate 
the figures for the “national greenhouse gas emissions” of both the base 
year and the target year on a net emissions basis, taking into account the 
“reduction performance achieved by utilizing carbon sinks etc.” so that 
both are based on net emissions.
From a scientific and policy perspective, applying different calculation 
standards to the base point and the target point makes it impossible to 
chart a curve that connects the two. The IPCC’s reports also use net 
emissions as a consistent basis when presenting emission reduction 
pathways related to “net zero.” If the term “national greenhouse gas 
emissions,” which appears twice in Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act, is interpreted such that gross emissions are 
used for the base year and net emissions for the target year, the resulting 
pathway to the 2050 carbon neutrality target would be based on 
inconsistent standards —for example, “2018 gross emissions –2030 net 
emissions –2050 net emissions.” This would prevent the management of 
a scientifically and policy-wise reasonable reduction pathway.
In addition, the figures for the land use sector under the Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue show that the absorption volume for the base 
year, 2018, is calculated as “–41.3” million tons, while the target for 
- 143 -2030 is set at “–26.7” million tons, indicating a decrease in absorption. 
The absorption targets for each year from 2023 to 2030 are also set to 
decrease steadily. Therefore, if the absorption volume is not reflected in 
the base year but is reflected only in the target year, this would create 
a distortion in which the “national greenhouse gas emissions” appear to 
have been reduced due to an increase in absorption, even though the 
actual absorption has declined. Such a distortion runs counter to the 
“transparency” pursued by the Paris Agreement and hinders efforts to 
strengthen actual reductions in other sectors.
Interpreting the term “national greenhouse gas emissions” in Article 8, 
Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act as referring to gross 
emissions for the base year, 2018, and net emissions for the target year, 
2030, is impermissible not only because it is inconsistent with the text, 
structure, and legislative purpose of Article 8, Section (1), but also 
because it represents an approach that fails to align with the scientific 
management of greenhouse gas reduction pathways and the transparency 
pursued by the Paris Agreement, thereby potentially undermining global 
efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
The 2030 national greenhouse gas emissions target set under the 
Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue falls short of the 40 
percent reduction rate established by Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement 
Decree, regardless of how the term “national greenhouse gas emissions” 
in Article 8, Section (1) is interpreted: if interpreted as net emissions, it 
results in a 36.4 percent reduction from 2018 levels; even if interpreted 
as gross emissions, it still amounts to only a 29.6 percent reduction.
Article 8, Section  (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act 
specifies how the mid- to long-term reduction target is to be set and 
merely delegates the concrete reduction ratio—within the range of not 
less than 35 percen t—to be prescribed by Presidential Decree. The 
Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue were adopted under Section (2) of 
- 144 -the same Article as a means of achieving the 2030 mid- to long-term 
reduction target established pursuant to Section  (1), and the Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue were adopted under Section  (3) of the same 
Article as a means of achieving both the mid- to long-term reduction 
target and the Sectoral Reduction Targets at Issue. Under the framework 
laid out in Article 8 of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, the 2030 
national greenhouse gas emissions target defined in the Sectoral and 
Annual Reduction Targets at Issue represents a 36.4 percent reduction 
from 2018 levels on a net emissions basis. While this satisfies the 
requirement of a reduction “of not less than 35 percent,” it falls short of 
the 40 percent reductio n—the rate prescribed by Presidential Decre e—
and therefore constitutes a target that does not fulfill the mid- to 
long-term reduction target under Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act.
As described above, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue are structured such that, when the 2030 national greenhouse gas 
emissions target derived therefrom is compared to the base year and the 
target year using the same basis—whether net emissions or gross 
emission s—it falls short of achieving the mid- to long-term reduction 
target for 2030 set forth in Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon 
Neutrality Framework Act and Article 3, Section (1) of its Enforcement 
Decree. This outcome is independent of the sectoral shares assigned or 
the shape of the reduction pathway. Thus, it is evident that the legally 
prescribed greenhouse gas reduction targets cannot result in actual 
reductions of the required magnitude. The Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue therefore fail to ensure effectiveness as a 
legal mechanism for regulating mitigation measures in response to the 
climate crisis.
3) Under the principle of administration based on the rule of law, all 
administrative actions are subject to the principle of statutory supremacy, 
which requires that such actions must not conflict with statute. In 
addition, the principle of statutory reservation requires that administrative 
- 145 -actions concerning essential matters have an express basis in statute. In 
this context, the final authority to interpret statutes lies with the judiciary 
under the principle of separation of powers.
In particular, the application of the principle of prohibition of 
insufficient protection to the institutional formation and implementation 
of protective measures in response to threats of fundamental rights 
infringement is grounded in deference to the legislature. It is 
impermissible for an executive authority, acting under legislative 
delegation, to arbitrarily alter the legislative scheme by interpreting the 
statute in a way that lowers the level of protection established by the 
legislature.
From the perspective of legally institutionalizing greenhouse gas 
reduction targets as protective measures commensurate with the 
dangerous situation of the climate crisis, once a framework of quantified 
reduction targets has been established by statute, any administrative plan 
that, on that basis, sets specific targets for emissions and removals by 
sector and by year constitutes an administrative action subject to the 
principle of statutory supremacy. The Sectoral and Annual Reduction 
Targets at Issue also fall within the scope of such administrative action. 
In this context, the final authority to interpret Article 8, Section (1) of 
the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act—which is applied to derive the 
method for calculating emissions target s—and Sections (2) and (3) of the 
same Articl e—which govern the relationship between the mid- to 
long-term reduction target and the sectoral and yearly reduction goals—
lies with the Court as the body responsible for judicial review.
However, the method adopted by the government in the Sectoral and 
Annual Reduction Targets at Issue—using the standard of “gross 
emissions for the base year–net emissions for the target year”—
constitutes, as previously discussed, an arbitrary alteration of the 
quantified reduction framework established by the legislature in Article 
8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act. By doing so, it 
- 146 -lowers the level of protective measures aimed at mitigating the climate 
crisis. Such an interpretation of the statute by the government is 
therefore unacceptable.
4) Therefore, the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, in 
terms of their method for calculating emissions targets, not only conflict 
with the text, structure, and legislative purpose of the Carbon Neutrality 
Framework Act, but also fail to meet the minimum characteristics 
required of a protective measure commensurate with the dangerous 
situation of the climate crisis. They fall short in light of i) scientific 
facts and international standards of conduct that must be taken into 
account in determining the numerical values of specific greenhouse gas 
reduction targets, and ii) institutional effectiveness as an administrative 
plan intended to achieve the reduction targets prescribed by statute and 
regulation. As such, they either violate the principle of prohibition of 
insufficient protection or constitute an administrative plan in which the 
government has arbitrarily altered the quantified reduction framework 
under Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, 
thereby lowering the level of protection and violating the principle of 
statutory supremacy in rule-of-law based administration.
In the management and advancement of greenhouse gas reduction 
measures, setting quantitative levels of reduction targets is important. 
Equally important, however, is accurately measuring those levels once 
they have been set. It is self-evident that if such measurements are 
distorted or improperly conducted, effective management of reduction 
measures becomes impossible.
Regarding the implementation status of the Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue, the Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth 
Commission released, in a press briefing dated April 7, 2024, estimated 
greenhouse gas emissions for the year 2023 in the four major sectors. 
The estimated figures, expressed in million tons, were as follows: 
power generation and conversion 203.7, industry 244.7, buildings 45.2, 
- 147 -and transportation 95.0. Although these are provisional estimates, they 
indicate that, in 2023 alone, emissions in just these four major sectors 
were reduced by approximately 32.3 million tons more than the annual 
target for that year under the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue. This amount exceeds the difference between the 2030 net 
emissions target under the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue—436.6 million tons—and the 2030 net emissions level that would 
reflect a 40 percent reduction from the 2018 level using a consistent net 
emissions basis for both the base year and target year—411.7 million 
tons.
There may be a variety of differing opinions from a policy perspective 
as to whether the specific numerical targets set in the Sectoral and 
Annual Reduction Targets at Issue are appropriate in light of each 
sector’s reduction potential, and judicial review based on such policy 
views would be difficult. However, at the very least, when formulating 
the administrative plan—which is the first step in implementing the 
policies needed to achieve the mid- to long-term reduction targets 
established under the relevant statute and regulatio n—questions such as 
whether the method used to calculate the specific emission reduction 
figures is inconsistent with the language and structure of the statute and 
regulation, and whether such inconsistency distorts the evaluation of 
reduction performance and thereby adversely affects global reduction 
efforts, are subject to judicial review.
5) The Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue infringe 
Complainants’ environmental right by violating the State’s duty to 
protect fundamental rights, in that the method of calculating the national 
greenhouse gas emissions target is inconsistent with the principle of 
prohibition of insufficient protection or the principle of statutory 
supremacy. 
However, if the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue were 
to immediately lose legal force, the individual greenhouse gas reduction 
- 148 -measures formulated on their basis would be deprived of their 
quantitative benchmarks, thereby resulting in a setback of the 
institutional framework for greenhouse gas reduction and creating an 
even more unconstitutional situation. 
In addition, in correcting the standards for calculating emissions in the 
base year and target year in accordance with the interpretation of Article 
8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, the government 
retains broad authority to formulate administrative plans regarding how 
to adjust the numerical emission targets for each sector and each year in 
specific terms.
Article 75, Section (3) of the Constitutional Court Act provides that “In 
the case referred to in Section (2), the Constitutional Court may annul the 
exercise of governmental power that caused the infringement of a 
fundamental right or declare the failure to act to be unconstitutional,” 
thereby presupposing that the Court has discretion as to whether to annul 
the exercise of governmental power when upholding a constitutional 
complaint. With respect to the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at 
Issue, it is warranted not to issue an annulment decision but instead to 
declare them unconstitutional, so that the Targets retain their legal effect 
until the government prepares and implements a revised plan that aligns 
the sectoral and yearly reduction goals with the purport of this decision, 
pursuant to its binding force. 
Therefore, with regard to the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets 
at Issue, the Court should render a decision with the holding declaring 
them unconstitutional on the ground that they infringe Complainants’ 
environmental right.
4. Sub-conclusion
The allocation of reduction burdens among the sectors and the 
resulting shape of the reduction trajectory from 2023 to 2030, as set 
- 149 -forth in the Sectoral and Annual Reduction Targets at Issue, cannot by 
themselves be deemed to violate the principle of prohibition of 
insufficient protection. 
With respect to the method used in the Sectoral and Annual Reduction 
Targets at Issue to calculate the national greenhouse gas emissions target
—specifically, the standard of “gross emissions for the base year–net 
emissions for the target year”—the rejecting opinion of Justices Lee 
Jongseok, Lee Eunae, Lee Youngjin, and Kim Hyungdu held that this 
did not violate the principle of prohibition of insufficient protection or 
the principle of statutory supremacy, whereas the unconstitutionality 
opinion of Justices Kim Kiyoung, Moon Hyungbae, Lee Mison, Jung 
Jungmi, and Cheong Hyungsik held that it did violate the principle of 
prohibition of insufficient protection or the principle of statutory 
supremacy and thereby infringed Complainants’ environmental right. 
VIII. Conclusion
Article 8, Section (1) of the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act does 
not conform to the Constitution, but it will remain in effect until 
amended by February 28, 2026. The claims against Article 3, Section (1) 
of the Enforcement Decree of the same Act and the Sectoral and Annual 
Reduction Targets at Issue are unjustified and are therefore rejected. The 
remaining claims of Complainants and the motions to intervene as a 
Complainant and in support of Complainants filed by Movant are 
dismissed as nonjusticiable. Accordingly, the Court issues the decision as 
set forth in the Holding.
With respect to the claim against the Sectoral and Annual Reduction 
Targets at Issue, Justices Lee Jongseok, Lee Eunae, Lee Youngjin, and 
Kim Hyungdu filed an opinion of rejection, while Justices Kim Kiyoung, 
Moon Hyungbae, Lee Mison, Jung Jungmi, and Cheong Hyungsik filed 
an opinion of unconstitutionality, finding that the Targets infringe 
Complainants’ environmental rights. Although the majority held that the 
- 150 -Targets are unconstitutional, the number of Justices supporting this 
view did not meet the quorum required for a decision upholding a 
constitutional complaint under Article 113, Section (1) of the 
Constitution and Article 23, Section (2), main clause, Item 1 of the 
Constitutional Court Act. Therefore, a decision of rejection is rendered. 
As for the remaining claims, the participating Justices were unanimous in 
their opinions. 
Justices Lee Jongseok (Presiding Justice), Lee Eunae, Lee Youngjin, 
Kim Kiyoung, Moon Hyungbae, Lee Mison, Kim Hyungdu, Jung 
Jungmi, and Cheong Hyungsik
